question_text: You are emailing a potential collaborator for the first time. Which greeting best balances respect and approachability?
historical_context: Reflects the shift from "Elite" Victorian formality to "Merit-based Etiquette," where communication became a tool for partnership rather than a display of rank.

options.text: "To whom it may concern, I hereby request a moment of your time."
options.motivation: This feels overly bureaucratic and creates too much distance. It suggests a "stiff" personality that might be difficult to work with.

options.text: "Hi [Name], I'm reaching out because I saw your work and wanted to chat."
options.motivation: While friendly, starting with "Hi" and "chat" for a first-time contact can be seen as too informal for a professional introduction.

options.text: "Dear [Name], I hope this email finds you well. I am writing to discuss a potential collaboration."
options.motivation: This is the gold standard. It uses a formal anchor ("Dear") but follows with a warm, professional purpose.

question_text: You notice you misspelled a client's name in a previous email. How do you fix it?
historical_context: In middle-class circles, "The Name" is the most important part of a person's brand. Acknowledging a misspelling is a sign of peer-to-peer respect.

options.text: Send a quick follow-up: "Apologies for the typo in your name, [Correct Name]. I’ve corrected my records."
options.motivation: This shows "Reliability." Admitting a small mistake immediately builds trust and shows attention to detail.

options.text: Ignore it and just spell it correctly next time.
options.motivation: This is safe, but it misses an opportunity to show that you are "verzorgd" (meticulous) about your professional mirror.

options.text: Send a long email explaining how tired you were and why you made the mistake.
options.motivation: Over-explaining personal "drama" lowers your professional standing and makes you seem disorganized.

question_text: You are in a meeting and want to show you are listening intently. What is your physical stance?
historical_context: The "Lean In" posture became a symbolic gesture of the "Active Professional" during the rise of service-based economies.

options.text: Slouching back in the chair with hands behind your head.
options.motivation: This signals "afstandelijke" (distanced) behavior. It suggests you think you are "above" the conversation.

options.text: Sitting upright, leaning slightly forward, and maintaining consistent eye contact.
options.motivation: This "active" posture radiates expertise and genuine interest without being aggressive.

options.text: Constantly taking notes on your phone.
options.motivation: Even if you are working, looking at a screen can be interpreted as being "distracted" rather than "engaged".

question_text: You are at a professional mixer. How do you introduce yourself to a stranger?
historical_context: In the "Public Arena," the ability to introduce oneself clearly is a marker of social mobility and self-assured merit.

options.text: "Hi, I'm [Name]. I do [Job Title] at [Company]. What brings you here?"
options.motivation: This is clear "Professional Branding." It provides context and immediately invites the other person to speak.

options.text: "I am the Lead Director of Global Operations. I've won three awards this year."
options.motivation: This is "opscheppen" (boasting). It focuses on status rather than connection, which is a middle-class taboo.

options.text: Just stand near them and wait for them to ask who you are.
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig" (submissive). It lacks the confidence expected in the "Publieke Arena".

question_text: A colleague asks for your opinion on a project direction. How do you respond to show authority?
historical_context: The language of "Advice" grew with the professional class to distinguish expert consultants from general laborers.

options.text: "I think that maybe we should try the other way."
options.motivation: "Think" and "maybe" are weakeners. They make your expertise sound like a guess.

options.text: "I advise proceeding with the other way because it aligns with our goals."
options.motivation: Using "I advise" or "I recommend" frames you as a consultant and an expert.

options.text: "Do whatever you want, it's not my project."
options.motivation: This is unprofessional and damages your "Reliability" as a team member.

question_text: A peer says, "That was an excellent presentation." How do you respond?
historical_context: In merit-based societies, it is acceptable to take credit for hard work, but it must be balanced with grace.

options.text: "It was nothing, I barely prepared."
options.motivation: False modesty can make it seem like you don't value your own "Quality" of work.

options.text: "Thank you, I’m glad you found the data useful. I put a lot of work into the analysis."
options.motivation: This acknowledges the "Merit" (the work) while being gracious. It reinforces your "Professional Brand".

options.text: "I know, I'm the best at this."
options.motivation: Arrogance breaks the "Social Navigation" and creates a gap between you and your peers.

question_text: You are in a quiet "Social Arena" (like a train or lobby) and your phone rings.
historical_context: Public space etiquette evolved to manage the friction of high-density urban living among the middle class.

options.text: Answer and speak loudly so everyone knows you are busy and important.
options.motivation: This is a violation of "Publieke Arena" rules. It is seen as "brash" and inconsiderate.

options.text: Let it go to voicemail, or step outside/to a quiet corner and speak in a lowered tone.
options.motivation: This shows you respect the shared space and understand "Social Navigation".

options.text: Answer and tell the person you'll call back in 10 minutes while staying in your seat.
options.motivation: Acceptable if brief, but still interrupts the "quiet" of the environment.

question_text: You are going to a Friday office lunch. The code is "Smart Casual."
historical_context: "Smart Casual" bridged the gap between the rigid uniforms of the past and the creative freedom of the modern workplace.

options.text: A suit with no tie.
options.motivation: Sometimes a bit too formal for "casual," making you look like you can't "relax".

options.text: Dark jeans or chinos with a crisp shirt and clean shoes.
options.motivation: This is the perfect "Middle Class" interpretation. It is "verzorgd" (neat) but approachable.

options.text: A track suit because it's Friday.
options.motivation: Too casual. It signals a lack of respect for the professional "Arena".

question_text: You see two colleagues talking at a coffee machine and want to join.
historical_context: The "Rule of Three" in social settings (it's easier to join a pair than a large group) is a foundational networking skill.

options.text: Stand a few feet away, wait for eye contact, and then step in.
options.motivation: This is "Social Navigation." You are looking for a non-verbal "invite" before entering the space.

options.text: Interrupt them mid-sentence with your own story.
options.motivation: This is seen as "onbeschoft" (rude) and shows a lack of social awareness.

options.text: Just stand right between them and wait for them to stop talking.
options.motivation: This is physically intrusive and creates discomfort.

question_text: You are finishing a call with a client. How do you leave a lasting "Reliability" impression?
historical_context: Professional closing remarks act as a "verbal contract," summarizing the value of the interaction.

options.text: "Okay, bye!" and hang up immediately.
options.motivation: A bit too abrupt. It leaves the "Professional Mirror" feeling unfinished.

options.text: "Thank you for your time. I will send the follow-up email by tomorrow morning. Have a great day."
options.motivation: This combines "Respect" with a "Merit-based" promise (the follow-up). It reinforces your "Brand".

options.text: Keep talking for another 10 minutes about your personal life.
options.motivation: This ignores the client's time and blurs the "Privé en Zakelijk" (Private and Business) boundary.

question_text: You arrive at a reception and see a group of three people in a closed circle talking. How do you join the conversation?
historical_context: Networking etiquette evolved from 18th-century "Salons," where the ability to move between groups defined one's social and professional mobility.

options.text: Stand nearby, wait for a natural pause and eye contact, then ask: "May I join you?"
options.motivation: This is effective "Social Navigation." You respect their space but show the confidence of a peer.

options.text: Walk directly into the center of the circle and start introducing yourself.
options.motivation: This is socially intrusive. It breaks the "Public Arena" flow and can be seen as aggressive.

options.text: Stand silently behind one of them and wait until they turn around and see you.
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig" (submissive) and can make others feel uncomfortable or "stalked."

question_text: You are hosting a business lunch with two clients in a Belgian bistro. The waiter arrives. Who speaks first?
historical_context: The "Host" role is a position of subtle power. In middle-class culture, the host ensures the "Quality" of the interaction by managing the environment.

options.text: Wait for the clients to realize the waiter is there and let them decide when to order.
options.motivation: While polite, it lacks "Professional Branding." As the host, you should facilitate the experience.

options.text: Take the lead: "I think we are ready. Shall we start with some water for the table?"
options.motivation: This shows you are in control of the "Public Arena" logistics, allowing your guests to relax.

options.text: Tell the waiter to come back later because you haven't finished your story.
options.motivation: This is seen as "low-status" behavior. It is disrespectful to the service staff and the time of your guests.

question_text: You had a great dinner with a client where you discussed both business and your shared love for hiking. What do you post on LinkedIn the next day?
historical_context: LinkedIn is the digital extension of the "Public Arena." Etiquette here mirrors the "Old Middle Class" rule: keep your public image polished and merit-based.

options.text: A photo of the expensive wine bottle and the bill to show how successful you are.
options.motivation: This violates "Materiële Bescheidenheid" (Material Modesty). Talking about costs is a major taboo.

options.text: A post about the professional insights gained, perhaps mentioning the "great environment" for the meeting.
options.motivation: This keeps the "Privé en Zakelijk" (Private/Business) balance. It focuses on "Quality" and professional growth.

options.text: A photo of the client eating their dessert with a funny caption.
options.motivation: Too informal. It risks the client’s "Professional Mirror" and can be seen as "unverzorgd" (unprofessional).

question_text: You are out for a "Smart Casual" dinner with 4 colleagues. The bill is not itemized. How do you handle payment?
historical_context: The "Dutch Treat" (splitting equally) became a middle-class standard to ensure no one felt "underdanig" (submissive) to a wealthy benefactor.

options.text: Suggest: "Shall we just split the bill equally to keep it simple?"
options.motivation: This reflects "Social Navigation" efficiency. It prioritizes the group's harmony over the exact cents.

options.text: Insist on calculating exactly who had the extra side of fries to the cent.
options.motivation: This can be seen as "petty" in a middle-class setting, where time and harmony are often valued more than small change.

options.text: Pay the whole bill and make a point of saying how much it cost.
options.motivation: This is "opscheppen" (boasting). It creates an uncomfortable "debt" for your peers.

question_text: You are talking to a peer about a complex topic. How do you stand?
historical_context: Open body language is a marker of democratic, merit-based societies where "transparency" is valued over "guarded" aristocratic poses.

options.text: Cross your arms tightly and look at your watch frequently.
options.motivation: This is "afstandelijke" (distanced) behavior. it signals you are bored or feel superior.

options.text: Feet shoulder-width apart, hands visible, nodding occasionally to show engagement.
options.motivation: This projects "expertise" and "interest." It shows you are an active participant in the "Public Arena."

options.text: Stand very close to them, almost touching shoulders, to show you are "best friends."
options.motivation: This invades personal space and ignores the "Social Navigation" boundaries of professional peers.

question_text: You are representing your company at a trade show. Which grooming choice most affects your "Reliability" score?
historical_context: Cleanliness became a middle-class obsession in the 19th century as a "silent code" to distinguish professionals from manual labor.

options.text: Wearing a very strong, expensive cologne or perfume.
options.motivation: In the middle-class matrix, "Limited accessories and scents" are the rule. Overpowering scents are seen as "try-hard."

options.text: Ensuring your hair is neat, your clothes are lint-free, and your shoes are clean.
options.motivation: These "details" signal "betrouwbaarheid" (reliability). It shows you are "verzorgd" (meticulous) about the small things.

options.text: Wearing a lot of gold jewelry to show you can afford it.
options.motivation: Flashy jewelry violates "Materiële Bescheidenheid." It draws attention to "wealth" rather than "merit."

question_text: A client sends you a non-urgent WhatsApp message on a Sunday evening. How do you respond?
historical_context: The rise of the digital "Public Arena" has made "Time Blocking" a modern status symbol of the organized, high-merit professional.

options.text: Answer immediately to show you are "always available."
options.motivation: This can signal a lack of boundaries. In the middle class, "Quality" of life is a sign of a successful professional.

options.text: Wait until Monday morning during professional hours to send a polished response.
options.motivation: This maintains the "Privé en Zakelijk" (Private/Business) boundary and protects your "Professional Brand."

options.text: Ignore it and never mention it again.
options.motivation: This is "unreliable." Even if you wait until Monday, you must eventually acknowledge the communication.

question_text: You are entering a corporate building. A person you don't know is walking a few steps behind you.
historical_context: "Civility" in public spaces (like holding doors) evolved as a way for strangers in dense urban environments to signal they are "safe" and "refined."

options.text: Hold the door open, make brief eye contact, and give a small nod.
options.motivation: This shows awareness of others in the "Public Arena." It is polite without being "underdanig" (submissive).

options.text: Let the door close behind you; it’s an automatic door anyway.
options.motivation: While practical, it can seem "unverzorgd" (uncaring) or socially blind.

options.text: Slam the door quickly to ensure no one follows you for security reasons.
options.motivation: Unless it's a high-security vault, this is seen as aggressive and paranoid. It ruins your "Social Navigation."

question_text: The conversation at the table turns to a controversial political topic. What do you do?
historical_context: "Table Talk" in the middle class has traditionally avoided "Religion and Politics" to ensure that business partnerships aren't ruined by personal ideology.

options.text: State your opinion loudly and tell everyone else why they are wrong.
options.motivation: This is a failure of "Adaptive Linguistics." It creates conflict in a "Social Arena" designed for harmony.

options.text: Listen, ask curious questions to understand others, and keep your own stance moderate.
options.motivation: This is "Social Navigation." You remain an active participant without becoming a source of friction.

options.text: Get up and leave the table in protest.
options.motivation: An overreaction that damages your "Professional Brand" as a stable, reliable peer.

question_text: You’ve been talking to someone for 10 minutes at a borrel (drinks) and want to move on. How do you leave?
historical_context: In networking, the "Exit" is as important as the "Entry." It defines the "last impression" of your "Professional Mirror."

options.text: Wait for them to take a breath, then say: "It's been a pleasure meeting you. I'm going to circulate a bit more, but here is my card/LinkedIn."
options.motivation: This is "Social Navigation" at its best. It validates the interaction while asserting your own agenda.

options.text: Just walk away the moment they look in another direction.
options.motivation: This is "ghosting" in person. It leaves a "unverzorgd" (unpolished) impression of your character.

options.text: Tell them: "You are talking too much, I need to find someone more interesting."
options.motivation: Brutal honesty is not part of "Adaptive Linguistics." It destroys your reputation in the "Public Arena."

question_text: You have just bought a high-quality German car. A colleague says, "That’s a beautiful car!" How do you respond?
historical_context: Middle-class wealth is historically "earned wealth." Etiquette dictates that you justify spending through logic (safety, durability) rather than prestige.

options.text: "Thanks! It was the top-of-the-line model and cost me a fortune."
options.motivation: Mentioning the cost directly is seen as "boasting." It violates the rule of material modesty.

options.text: "Thanks! I’ve found it to be very reliable and the build quality is excellent for long commutes."
options.motivation: Using "Quality" and "Reliability" as descriptors justifies the purchase through merit and utility rather than status.

options.text: "It’s just a basic vehicle, nothing special at all."
options.motivation: Excessive false modesty can seem insincere. It is better to acknowledge the quality of your choice.

question_text: At a professional dinner, an acquaintance asks you exactly how much you earn "net" per month.
historical_context: The taboo on salary stems from a desire to maintain the appearance of equality among peers, preventing financial competition from ruining social bonds.

options.text: Give them the exact number and ask what they earn in return.
options.motivation: In the middle class, you talk about your "job," but almost never about your exact salary. This is a major taboo.

options.text: Say, "I am comfortably compensated for my level of responsibility. How is your current project going?"
options.motivation: This maintains modesty by pivoting the conversation from money back to professional "Merit" and "Responsibility."

options.text: "That is a very private question and I don't appreciate you asking."
options.motivation: While the question is invasive, an aggressive response breaks the social harmony of the "Public Arena."

question_text: You just returned from an expensive, luxury safari. A colleague asks how it was.
historical_context: For the middle class, vacations are framed as "Cultural Enrichment" or "Self-Improvement" rather than just a display of leisure.

options.text: "It was incredibly expensive, but the five-star service was worth every penny."
options.motivation: Using "expensive" as your primary descriptor is seen as brash and "low-status" behavior.

options.text: "It was a fantastic experience. The level of detail in the tours and the quality of the nature conservation were impressive."
options.motivation: This focuses on "Quality" and "Experience." You are showing that you value the depth of the trip over the price tag.

options.text: "It was okay, just a standard trip to Africa."
options.motivation: Dismissing a great experience can make you seem "distant" or ungrateful for your own success.

question_text: A business partner admires your designer watch. How do you acknowledge it?
historical_context: "Inconspicuous Consumption" is the hallmark of the established middle class; you buy things that last rather than things that shout.

options.text: "Thank you. I really value the craftsmanship and the fact that it is built to last a lifetime."
options.motivation: You are using "Craftsmanship" as a codeword for value. This allows you to acknowledge success without being crass.

options.text: "Thanks, I bought it to celebrate hitting my sales targets this year."
options.motivation: Tying a purchase directly to a specific financial bonus can feel a bit too "transactional" for some circles.

options.text: "It cost me five thousand dollars, but it’s a good investment."
options.motivation: Directly discussing the price tag is the fastest way to lose the "Professional Mirror" of a refined peer.

question_text: You are asking your manager for a salary increase. Which framing is most effective?
historical_context: Professional negotiations are viewed as a "Partnership." You are discussing the market value of your "Merit."

options.text: "I need more money because my rent and cost of living have increased recently."
options.motivation: This is "needs-based." Middle-class negotiations should be "merit-based."

options.text: "Based on the quality of my deliverables and the increase in my responsibilities, I’d like to discuss a compensation adjustment."
options.motivation: This ties your request to "Quality" and "Competence," which are the core values of the middle-class matrix.

options.text: "I know how much my colleague makes, and I want at least that much."
options.motivation: Comparing yourself directly to others using raw numbers is seen as unprofessional and aggressive.

question_text: You want to send a gift to a client to thank them for a partnership. What do you choose?
historical_context: Gift-giving among peers is a way to cement "Merit-based" relationships. The focus is always on the quality of the gesture.

options.text: A gold-plated ornament with your company logo in large letters.
options.motivation: This is flashy and lacks "Materiële Bescheidenheid." It feels like an advertisement rather than a gift.

options.text: A high-quality local product (e.g., fine chocolates or wine) with a handwritten note.
options.motivation: This focuses on "Quality" and "Personal Detail." It shows you have good taste without trying too hard to impress.

options.text: An envelope with a cash tip.
options.motivation: In a professional context, cash is seen as crude and potentially violates ethical boundaries.

question_text: You have just moved into a larger house. How do you describe it to your team?
historical_context: In many European cultures, "The Home" is a private sanctuary. One shares the function of the home, but keeps the value private.

options.text: "We finally moved into a neighborhood that reflects our professional standing."
options.motivation: This sounds "elite-distanced" and arrogant. It emphasizes social rank over personal comfort.

options.text: "It’s a great space. We really appreciate having more room for the family and the quality of the local schools."
options.motivation: This is relatable and practical. It focuses on the "Utility" of the home rather than the prestige.

options.text: "It's just a house, I don't really care about where I live."
options.motivation: This "false indifference" can seem fake, especially if the move was a significant life event.

question_text: Your client is paying for lunch and says, "Order whatever you like." What is your strategy?
historical_context: Ordering is a social dance. By matching the host, you signal that you are "in sync" with their professional energy.

options.text: Order the most expensive item on the menu to show you have expensive taste.
options.motivation: This is greedy and shows a lack of "Social Navigation." It disregards the host's resources.

options.text: Order a mid-range item that matches the host’s ordering style.
options.motivation: This is the "Professional Mirror." It shows you are a peer who understands the context of the environment.

options.text: Order the cheapest thing on the menu to show how frugal you are.
options.motivation: This can make the host feel uncomfortable or make it seem like you aren't enjoying the venue.

question_text: Your company has had its most profitable year ever. How do you announce this internally?
historical_context: Modern corporate culture emphasizes "Purpose-driven Success," where the work is the primary goal and the money is the reward for doing it well.

options.text: "We made record profits! Everyone is going to be rich!"
options.motivation: This focuses entirely on "Raw Money," which can feel crass and demotivating for long-term goals.

options.text: "This success is a testament to the quality of our work and our reliability as a partner. Thank you for your commitment."
options.motivation: This frames the profit as a byproduct of "Merit" and "Quality." it reinforces the company's core values.

options.text: Say nothing about the profit and just give everyone a small gift card.
options.motivation: Transparency is valued in the middle class. Ignoring the success can lead to a lack of trust.

question_text: You are buying a new suit or outfit for a major conference. What do you look for?
historical_context: Refinement in the middle class is often about "The Hidden Detail"—quality that is felt and seen by peers, but not shouted to the public.

options.text: Something with the designer's logo clearly visible on the outside.
options.motivation: Visible logos are often seen as "brash" and a violation of "Materiële Bescheidenheid."

options.text: A well-tailored piece made of high-quality fabric with no visible branding.
options.motivation: This is the "Inconspicuous Mirror." It signals expertise and attention to detail to those who understand quality.

options.text: The cheapest possible option because "clothes don't make the man."
options.motivation: Being "unverzorgd" (unkept) suggests you might be "unreliable" in your professional work as well.

question_text: Your manager suggests a strategy during a meeting that you know, based on your expertise, will fail. How do you respond?
historical_context: Modern "Boardroom Etiquette" was designed to allow for "Safe Disagreement," ensuring the best ideas win without causing social warfare or loss of face.

options.text: "That’s a bad idea and I don’t think it will work for the company."
options.motivation: This is too blunt and confrontational. It triggers defensiveness and ruins the collaborative "Mirror" of the room.

options.text: "I hear your point. However, looking at the technical data, I’m concerned about the long-term ROI. Could we explore an alternative together?"
options.motivation: This uses "Diplomatic Softening." You validate the person first, then use "Quality" and "Data" (ROI) to pivot toward a better solution.

options.text: Agree with the idea in the meeting, but then don't execute it later.
options.motivation: This is "Unreliable." Reliability is the core pillar of the middle class; being passive-aggressive destroys your merit-based brand.

question_text: You are presenting your findings when a peer constantly interrupts you with their own anecdotes. How do you regain control?
historical_context: Professional middle-class interactions value "Efficiency." Keeping a meeting on track is seen as a sign of high-level competence.

options.text: Stop talking immediately and glare at them until they finish.
options.motivation: This is "distanced" and awkward. It makes the tension in the "Public Arena" visible to everyone.

options.text: "That’s an interesting point, [Name]. I’d love to dive into that during the Q&A, but let me finish this section first to keep us on track."
options.motivation: This is "Social Navigation." You acknowledge them but firmly redirect back to the "Quality" of the presentation.

options.text: Shout over them so that your voice is the only one heard.
options.motivation: This is seen as "brash" and a loss of emotional control, which signals low "Reliability."

question_text: A client arrives 20 minutes late to a lunch you are hosting, offering no apology. How do you handle the start of the meal?
historical_context: The "Graceful Host" is a middle-class ideal that prioritizes the "Social Arena's" harmony over personal grievances.

options.text: Make a sarcastic comment about how "nice of them to show up."
options.motivation: This ruins the "Social Navigation." You lose the high ground and make the environment uncomfortable.

options.text: "I'm glad you could make it! I’ve already taken the liberty of ordering some water/bread for the table. Shall we look at the mains?"
options.motivation: This shows you are the "Host" in control. You move past the error gracefully to focus on the "Quality" of the meeting.

options.text: Spend the first 10 minutes explaining how their lateness has ruined your schedule.
options.motivation: This makes you look "stressed" rather than "reliable." It focuses on the "mess" instead of the "business."

question_text: You realize you sent an incorrect budget proposal to a major stakeholder. How do you fix it?
historical_context: Accountability became a core middle-class value during the Industrial Revolution; your "word" and your "data" are your bond.

options.text: Hope they don't notice and fix it in the next quarterly report.
options.motivation: This is a total failure of "Reliability." If found out, your "Merit-based" brand is permanently damaged.

options.text: Call/Email immediately: "I’ve identified a calculation error in the previous document. I’m sending the corrected version now to ensure our data is 100% accurate."
options.motivation: This is "Radical Accountability." You frame the correction as a commitment to "Quality," which actually increases trust.

options.text: Blame the junior assistant who did the spreadsheet.
options.motivation: Passing the blame is seen as "low status." A leader in the middle-class matrix takes responsibility for the output of their team.

question_text: A partner is pushing you to sign a contract immediately, but you need more time to review the details.
historical_context: The "Professional Pause" is a tactic used by the educated middle class to signal that they prioritize "Due Diligence" over impulsive action.

options.text: "I won't be pressured into this; stop being so aggressive."
options.motivation: This is too emotional. It breaks the "Professional Mirror" of a calm, collected expert.

options.text: "I appreciate the urgency. However, to maintain our standard of quality, I need to complete a final review. I’ll have an answer for you by [Time]."
options.motivation: You use "Quality" as a shield. It makes your delay sound like a service to them rather than a lack of decisiveness.

options.text: Sign it anyway to avoid the awkwardness of saying no.
options.motivation: This is "submissive" (onderdanig). It shows you lack the "Authority" required for high-level middle-class roles.

question_text: During a conference, someone publicly challenges your expertise in a rude manner.
historical_context: Public debate etiquette evolved to ensure that "the argument" stays central, while "the person" stays respected.

options.text: Insult their credentials in front of the audience.
options.motivation: This makes you look "unverzorgd" (unpolished). You lose the respect of the "Public Arena."

options.text: "That’s a provocative perspective. I’d be happy to share the specific research that led to my conclusion after the session."
options.motivation: This is "Adaptive Linguistics." You remain polite but firm, moving the "Conflict" to a private space to protect the public vibe.

options.text: Ignore the question and move to the next person immediately.
options.motivation: This can look like you are "hiding" or lack the "Merit" to defend your work.

question_text: A high-value client consistently mispronounces a technical term or a name. What do you do?
historical_context: "Subtle Correction" is a hallmark of the refined middle class, where the goal is to educate without embarrassing.

options.text: Correct them loudly every single time they say it.
options.motivation: This is pedantic and ruins "Social Navigation." It makes the client feel "underdanig" (inferior).

options.text: Simply use the correct pronunciation yourself in your next sentence without drawing attention to their mistake.
options.motivation: This is the "Professional Mirror." You provide the correct "Quality" without shaming the other party.

options.text: Start using the wrong pronunciation yourself so they feel comfortable.
options.motivation: This lowers your own "Professional Brand." You should remain the standard of "Expertise."

question_text: A business contact invites you to a personal event (like a wedding) that you do not wish to attend.
historical_context: The "Prior Commitment" is the classic middle-class polite refusal; it allows for a "No" without a "Rejection."

options.text: "I don’t think our relationship is at that level yet."
options.motivation: This is cold and "afstandelijk" (distanced). It creates a permanent wall between you and the contact.

options.text: "I’m so honored to be invited. Unfortunately, I have a prior commitment that weekend, but I’d love to grab a coffee soon to hear all about it."
options.motivation: This protects the "Privé en Zakelijk" (Private/Business) boundary while maintaining the "Professional Brand" of being likable and respectful.

options.text: Just don't RSVP and hope they forget.
options.motivation: This is "Unreliable." In the middle class, failing to respond is a sign of poor "Social Navigation."

question_text: A colleague takes credit for your work during a meeting with the boss.
historical_context: Professional ownership of ideas is the foundation of the middle-class "Promotion" system. Reclaiming credit is a necessary survival skill.

options.text: "Actually, [Name], I'm glad you liked the report I wrote. I spent weeks on that analysis—what was your favorite part?"
options.motivation: This is "Merit-based Etiquette." You reclaim the work by asking a technical question only the creator could answer, doing so politely.

options.text: Stand up and call them a liar in front of the boss.
options.motivation: This makes you seem unstable. It ruins the "Social Arena" and makes the boss uncomfortable.

options.text: Say nothing and wait until you can send an angry email later.
options.motivation: This is "Passive." Merit-based societies require you to (politely) stand up for your own "Quality" of work.

question_text: The negotiation has been tough, and no agreement was reached today. How do you leave the room?
historical_context: In diplomacy, "The Long Game" is everything. The middle class learned that today's opponent is often tomorrow's partner.

options.text: Storm out and slam the door.
options.motivation: This is a complete failure of "Social Navigation." You close the door on all future "Reliability."

options.text: "We’ve made some progress on understanding our different positions. Let’s take some time to reflect on the 'Quality' of these options and reconvene on [Date]."
options.motivation: You maintain the "Professional Mirror." You frame the stalemate as "Progress" and "Reflection," keeping the relationship alive.

options.text: Apologize for being so difficult and give in to all their demands.
options.motivation: This is "submissive." It destroys your "Professional Brand" as a strong, merit-based negotiator.

question_text: A project you led has failed publicly, costing the company a significant client. How do you address the department?
historical_context: In meritocratic societies, the ability to "own" a failure is often what separates middle-management from true executive leadership.

options.text: "The external vendors failed to deliver on time, and the market shifted unexpectedly."
options.motivation: While perhaps true, this focuses on "Excuses." In the middle class, this lowers your "Reliability" score because you aren't owning the outcome.

options.text: "I take full responsibility for this outcome. We have analyzed the 'Quality' gaps, and I have a plan to ensure our 'Reliability' is restored in the next quarter."
options.motivation: This is "Radical Accountability." It reinforces your brand as a leader who values merit and solution-oriented thinking over ego.

options.text: Avoid the office for a week and hope someone else explains it.
options.motivation: This is "Unreliable" behavior. It destroys your "Professional Mirror" and suggests you cannot handle high-stakes pressure.

question_text: You are overwhelmed and your "Quality" of work is slipping. How do you tell your supervisor?
historical_context: The "Work-Life Balance" is a modern middle-class construct. Success is seen as a marathon, not a sprint, requiring careful management of one's "Human Capital."

options.text: "I’m completely burnt out and I can't do this anymore."
options.motivation: Too emotional for the "Professional Arena." It emphasizes the "personal mess" rather than the "professional solution."

options.text: "To maintain the 'Quality' of my deliverables, I need to discuss reprioritizing my current workload. I want to ensure my 'Reliability' remains at its peak."
options.motivation: You frame your needs as a service to the company’s standards. You are protecting the "Brand" rather than just complaining.

options.text: Say nothing and keep working until you make a catastrophic mistake.
options.motivation: This ignores the "Reliability" pillar. Middle-class ethics value the "Sustainable Professional" who knows their limits.

question_text: You discover a colleague is "padding" their expense reports slightly. It doesn't affect you directly. What do you do?
historical_context: Middle-class ethics are built on "The Social Contract"—the idea that everyone must follow the same rules for the system to remain fair and merit-based.

options.text: Report them to HR immediately to show you are the "more moral" employee.
options.motivation: This can be seen as "underhanded" and damages your "Social Navigation" with peers. It’s a very sensitive "Arena" move.

options.text: Mention it to them privately: "I noticed a discrepancy in the reports. To protect our team's 'Reliability' and reputation, we should probably ensure that is corrected."
options.motivation: You act as a "Peer Mentor." You frame the correction as protecting the collective "Merit" of the team.

options.text: Start padding your own reports since everyone else is doing it.
options.motivation: This destroys your "Merit-based" standing. Once your integrity is gone, your middle-class status is compromised.

question_text: An old, slightly offensive social media post of yours from 10 years ago is rediscovered by a colleague.
historical_context: In the digital age, your "Online Arena" is an extension of your "Public Arena." Consistency and growth are the keys to reputation.

options.text: "I was a different person then, and that post does not reflect the 'Quality' of my character or my professional values today."
options.motivation: This is "Adaptive Linguistics." You acknowledge the past but pivot immediately to your current "Professional Brand."

options.text: Delete your entire social media presence and pretend it never happened.
options.motivation: This looks suspicious and "unreliable." It suggests you are hiding something rather than growing.

options.text: Tell the colleague to "mind their own business" and get angry.
options.motivation: Defensive aggression breaks the "Social Navigation" rules. It makes the situation a bigger conflict than it needs to be.

question_text: A close friend you helped hire is not meeting the company's "Quality" standards. You are their supervisor.
historical_context: The middle class replaced "Family Lineage" with "Competence" as the primary social marker. Loyalty to the "Standard" must come before loyalty to the "Person."

options.text: Give them a pass because they are your friend.
options.motivation: This is "Nepotism," the opposite of "Merit-based Etiquette." It destroys your "Reliability" with the rest of the team.

options.text: "As your friend, I want you to succeed. As your lead, I need to see the 'Quality' of your output reach [Level] to keep our team's 'Reliability' intact."
options.motivation: You clearly separate the "Privé en Zakelijk" (Private and Business) boundaries while offering a path to improvement.

options.text: Fire them immediately without warning to prove you aren't biased.
options.motivation: Too harsh. It lacks the "Social Navigation" required to maintain human relationships within a professional matrix.

question_text: A junior employee tags you in a post showing you both at an extremely expensive, "flashy" party during a time the company is cutting costs.
historical_context: The "Solidarity of the Middle Class" requires leaders to appear as if they are "in the trenches" with their workers, especially during lean times.

options.text: Untag yourself and ask the employee to remove it: "We need to maintain 'Materiële Bescheidenheid' during this period to show solidarity with the team."
options.motivation: This protects the "Public Arena" image of the company and shows you understand "Social Navigation."

options.text: Comment on the post about how much money you spent to show off.
options.motivation: Violates the taboo of "opscheppen" (boasting). It makes you look "Elite-distanced" and out of touch with your team.

options.text: Ignore it; what you do in your private time is your business.
options.motivation: While legally true, in the middle-class matrix, the "Public Arena" is always watching. Perception is part of your "Reliability."

question_text: A negotiation has turned personal, and the other party has insulted your professional "Merit."
historical_context: Professionalism is the "mask" that allows business to continue even when personal feelings are hurt.

options.text: Insult them back even harder to defend your honor.
options.motivation: This is a total loss of "Adaptive Linguistics." You have descended into a "low-status" conflict.

options.text: "It seems we’ve reached an emotional impasse. Let's pause here and return to the data when we can both focus on the 'Quality' of the outcome."
options.motivation: This is the "Professional Pause." You take the high ground and move the conflict back to "Merit" and "Reliability."

options.text: Cry and leave the room.
options.motivation: This is seen as a lack of "Professional Branding." Emotional regulation is a key middle-class competence.

question_text: You accidentally overhear a conversation about upcoming layoffs. How do you handle this "Private" information in the "Public Arena"?
historical_context: Discretion is a core "Middle Class" value. Knowing what not to say is as important as knowing what to say.

options.text: Tell all your work friends immediately so they can prepare.
options.motivation: While kind, this is "Unreliable" behavior regarding company confidentiality. It marks you as a "leaker."

options.text: Keep the information private but use the insight to ensure your own "Quality" and "Reliability" are more visible than ever.
options.motivation: This is "Strategic Social Navigation." You protect the professional boundary while securing your own merit-based position.

options.text: Use the information to blackmail your boss for a raise.
options.motivation: This is unethical and "low-status." It will eventually destroy your entire professional career.

question_text: You realize you have arrived at a "Formal" dinner in "Smart Casual" attire. Everyone else is in suits.
historical_context: In "The Public Arena," being "under-dressed" is a common fear. The etiquette fix is to acknowledge it briefly and then ignore it completely.

options.text: Make a joke about it immediately: "I seem to have missed the suit memo! I hope my 'Merit' can make up for my lack of a tie tonight."
options.motivation: Using humor to acknowledge the error removes the awkwardness and pivots the focus back to your "Competence."

options.text: Hide in the bathroom for the entire evening.
options.motivation: This is "submissive" and lacks the confidence of a middle-class peer.

options.text: Pretend you are so rich and important that you don't need to follow dress codes.
options.motivation: This is "Elite Arrogance." In the middle-class matrix, following the "Social Contract" of the group is expected.

question_text: You promoted "Employee A" over "Employee B." "Employee B" (who is better liked socially) is furious.
historical_context: The middle class relies on the "fairness of the game." Decisions must always be defensible through objective "Merit."

options.text: "I’m sorry, the boss made me do it."
options.motivation: This is "weak leadership." It shows you have no authority and are "unreliable" as a manager.

options.text: "This decision was based on the 'Quality' and consistency of output. Let’s look at your 'Reliability' metrics so we can get you ready for the next opening."
options.motivation: This is "Merit-based Etiquette." You use data to justify the decision and provide a path for the other person to improve.

options.text: Tell "Employee B" they are just not good enough and should quit.
options.motivation: Unnecessarily cruel. It fails in "Social Navigation" and creates a toxic "Arena."

question_text: You have an interview at 10:00 AM. What is the "Middle Class" standard for arrival time to show reliability?
historical_context: In the industrial and professional age, punctuality became the "silent handshake" of the middle class—a way to prove you were disciplined enough for high-responsibility work.

options.text: Arrive at 09:30 AM and wait in the lobby so they see you are very eager.
options.motivation: While enthusiastic, arriving too early can be perceived as a lack of "Social Navigation." You might disrupt the interviewer’s schedule or look desperate.

options.text: Arrive at the building at 09:45 AM, but walk into the reception at 09:53 AM.
options.motivation: This is the perfect balance. It shows you are organized and respect the other person's time without being intrusive.

options.text: Arrive exactly at 10:00 AM or 10:01 AM.
options.motivation: In the middle-class matrix, "On time is late." Arriving at the exact minute suggests you haven't accounted for potential delays, which lowers your "Reliability" score.

question_text: You are 22 and starting your first "real" job. What should be the priority for your public social media profiles?
historical_context: The separation of "Home" and "Public Life" is a 19th-century middle-class invention. In the 21st century, digital privacy settings are the new "fences" around our private gardens.

options.text: Keep all profiles public so recruiters can see your "authentic" social life.
options.motivation: This violates the "Professional Mirror." If your private life (parties, hobbies) is too visible, it can distract from your professional "Merit."

options.text: Set private accounts to "Strict" and create a clean, professional LinkedIn profile with a clear headshot.
options.motivation: This manages the "Privé en Zakelijk" (Private/Business) boundary. You control exactly what "Brand" you project to the professional world.

options.text: Delete all social media entirely.
options.motivation: In the modern "Public Arena," having no digital footprint can actually make you look "unreliable" or suspicious to younger recruiters.

question_text: You are in a meeting with colleagues aged 40+. A great idea is presented. How do you express your approval?
historical_context: Linguists call this "Code-Switching." The middle class has historically used more formal language to distance themselves from perceived "unrefined" social groups.

options.text: "That’s fire, I’m totally down for that!"
options.motivation: Using heavy slang (like "fire") with older peers can make you seem "onderdanig" (juvenile/inferior) rather than a professional peer.

options.text: "I agree. I think that approach aligns perfectly with our current goals."
options.motivation: This uses "Adaptive Linguistics." You show you can speak the "language of the office," which increases your perceived expertise.

options.text: Say nothing and just give a "thumbs up" emoji or gesture.
options.motivation: Too passive. Level 1 requires you to use verbal communication to establish your "Professional Brand."

question_text: The HR manual says the dress code is "Casual." It is your first week. What do you wear?
historical_context: The "Uniform" of the middle class has shifted from the suit to "Smart/Business Casual," but the goal remains the same: looking like someone who can be trusted with responsibility.

options.text: Your most comfortable hoodie and sweatpants.
options.motivation: "Casual" for a 20-year-old and "Casual" for a 50-year-old manager are often different. This risks looking "unverzorgd" (unkept).

options.text: One level above the minimum: A clean polo or shirt, dark jeans/chinos, and neat sneakers.
options.motivation: The "Plus-One" rule. By dressing slightly better than required, you signal "Reliability" and respect for the new "Arena."

options.text: A full suit and tie every day.
options.motivation: This is "over-dressing." It shows you haven't mastered "Social Navigation" and might not fit in with the team culture.

question_text: Your boss gives you a task, but you don't understand the technical instructions.
historical_context: The "Junior-Senior" relationship in the middle class is built on "Mentorship." Asking the right questions is a sign of high potential merit.

options.text: Pretend you understand, leave the room, and try to Google it later.
options.motivation: This leads to errors, which destroys your "Reliability." It is better to be clear early than wrong later.

options.text: "To ensure I deliver the 'Quality' you’re looking for, could you clarify the specific requirements for [Part X]?"
options.motivation: This frames your question as a commitment to "Quality." It makes you look like a meticulous professional rather than someone who is "lost."

options.text: Just say, "I don't get it, can you explain that again?"
options.motivation: While honest, it’s a bit "onderdanig" (submissive). Reframing it around the "result" is more "Middle Class."

question_text: You receive a work email at 6:00 PM on a Tuesday. You are out with friends.
historical_context: As communication moved from letters (days) to emails (seconds), the middle class had to invent "Digital Boundaries" to prevent work from consuming private life.

options.text: Stop everything and reply immediately from your phone.
options.motivation: This can lead to typos and shows a lack of "Social Navigation" boundaries.

options.text: If it's not an emergency, wait until Wednesday morning at 08:30 AM to send a polished, well-thought-out reply.
options.motivation: This demonstrates "Reliability" and "Quality" control. It also sets a professional boundary between "Privé en Zakelijk."

options.text: Delete the email and wait for them to ask you again.
options.motivation: This is a total failure of "Professional Branding." It marks you as someone who cannot be relied upon.

question_text: You are the youngest person in a group of professionals. How do you introduce yourself?
historical_context: Modern networking is "Peers-based." Even if you are junior, you are expected to navigate the social space with the poise of a future leader.

options.text: "Hi, I'm the new intern, sorry to bother you guys."
options.motivation: Apologizing for your presence is "onderdanig" (submissive). It suggests you don't belong in the "Arena."

options.text: "Hi, I'm [Name]. I recently joined the [Department] team. It’s a pleasure to meet you."
options.motivation: This is confident and clear. You state your "Merit" (your role) without being arrogant.

options.text: Don't say anything until someone specifically asks for your name.
options.motivation: This makes you "invisible." To move up in the middle-class matrix, you must be a visible, active participant.

question_text: You use the last of the coffee or the milk in the shared office kitchen.
historical_context: The "Shared Commons" of an office is a test of character. Middle-class etiquette prizes "Civility"—the ability to live and work together smoothly.

options.text: Leave the empty container there; that's the cleaning staff's job.
options.motivation: This is "Low Status" behavior. It shows a lack of respect for the shared "Public Arena" and your colleagues.

options.text: Refill the coffee or replace the milk immediately.
options.motivation: This small act signals "Reliability" and "Awareness." People notice who leaves a mess and who maintains the "Quality" of the environment.

options.text: Write a long, angry note on the fridge about how someone should have bought more milk.
options.motivation: This is seen as "petty" and creates unnecessary friction in the social group.

question_text: You are in a 30-minute meeting. Your phone vibrates in your pocket.
historical_context: Attention is the new "Wealth." Giving someone your full attention is one of the highest compliments in the modern middle-class etiquette.

options.text: Pull it out and check the notification under the table.
options.motivation: Everyone can see you doing this. It signals that the "Public Arena" (the meeting) is less important than your "Private Arena."

options.text: Keep it in your pocket or face down on the table and ignore it until the meeting ends.
options.motivation: This projects "Focus" and "Respect." It reinforces your "Professional Mirror" as someone who is fully present.

options.text: Answer the phone and say "I'm in a meeting" then hang up.
options.motivation: The interruption itself is the problem. It’s better to maintain the flow of the "Expert" environment.

question_text: A senior colleague corrects your grammar or a small detail in a report you wrote.
historical_context: In the middle class, "Feedback" is viewed as a "Gift" that helps you move up the meritocratic ladder.

options.text: Get defensive and explain why your way was actually okay.
options.motivation: This shows a lack of "Growth Mindset." It makes you look difficult to train.

options.text: "Thank you for catching that. I appreciate the feedback—I want to ensure the 'Quality' of our output is as high as possible."
options.motivation: This is "Merit-based Etiquette." You show that you care more about the "Work" than your "Ego."

options.text: Say "My bad" and walk away.
options.motivation: "My bad" is too informal for a professional "Correction" moment. It lacks the "Refinement" of a rising professional.

question_text: It is Friday afternoon, and the team is going for drinks. You are the newest and youngest member. How do you handle your beverage choice?
historical_context: The "After-Work Drink" is a European middle-class tradition used to build "Social Capital." It is a test of how you handle "Privé en Zakelijk" (Private and Business) boundaries.

options.text: Order the most expensive cocktail on the menu since the company is paying.
options.motivation: This violates "Material Modesty." It makes you look greedy and disconnected from the "Social Navigation" of the group.

options.text: Order a modest drink (beer, wine, or soda) and keep your consumption to a maximum of two.
options.motivation: This protects your "Professional Mirror." It shows you can socialize without losing your "Reliability" or self-control.

options.text: Drink as much as possible to show everyone how "fun" you are.
options.motivation: This is a high-risk move. In the middle-class matrix, being "the life of the party" often comes at the cost of being seen as a "serious professional."

question_text: You meet an interesting senior professional at a networking event. How do you ensure the connection lasts?
historical_context: In the past, the physical business card was a "Passport of Merit." Today, a LinkedIn request serves the same function of verifying your professional identity.

options.text: Ask them to follow your personal Instagram immediately.
options.motivation: Too personal. Instagram is part of the "Private Arena." Mixing it too early with professional contacts can damage your "Brand."

options.text: "It was great meeting you. May I add you on LinkedIn to stay updated on your work?"
options.motivation: This is the correct "Social Navigation." LinkedIn is the "Public Digital Arena" where professional merit is shared and tracked.

options.text: Wait for them to ask for your contact details first.
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig" (submissive). As a rising professional, you should show the initiative of a future peer.

question_text: You are out for lunch with three colleagues. The bill comes. No one has taken the lead yet.
historical_context: The "Split Bill" represents the middle-class ideal of equality and independence. It ensures that no one is "indebted" to another in a way that clouds professional judgment.

options.text: Sit quietly and look at your phone until someone else handles it.
options.motivation: This makes you look "unreliable" or financially struggling, which can subtly impact how peers view your professional success.

options.text: "Shall we just split this four ways to keep it simple?"
options.motivation: Even if you are the most junior, suggesting an efficient solution shows you understand "Social Navigation" and respect everyone's time.

options.text: Insist on paying for everyone to impress them.
options.motivation: This can be seen as "opscheppen" (boasting) and might make senior colleagues feel uncomfortable.

question_text: You find yourself alone in the elevator with the CEO.
historical_context: The "Elevator Pitch" is a survival skill for the rising middle class, where brief "Arena" moments can define a reputation.

options.text: Stare at your shoes and say nothing to avoid being annoying.
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig" (submissive). You miss an opportunity to show you can navigate the "Public Arena" with confidence.

options.text: "Good morning. I’m [Name] from the [X] department. We’re working on the [Y] project today—it’s going quite well."
options.motivation: This is "Professional Branding." You provide your name and a "Merit" hook (the project) while remaining brief and respectful.

options.text: Ask them how much money they make or what car they drive.
options.motivation: A massive violation of "Material Modesty" taboos. It marks you as someone who doesn't understand "Middle Class" boundaries.

question_text: A digital meeting is scheduled for 2:00 PM. When do you click the link?
historical_context: Digital etiquette has replaced physical "waiting room" etiquette. Being ready early signals that you respect the "Merit" of the meeting and the participants.

options.text: Exactly at 2:00 PM.
options.motivation: If you have technical issues, you will be late. In the digital world, being "on time" is the bare minimum.

options.text: At 1:58 PM, with your camera on and a professional background ready.
options.motivation: This shows "Reliability" and that you are "verzorgd" (prepared). It ensures you are ready to start the "Public Arena" interaction immediately.

options.text: At 2:05 PM with a joke about "bad Wi-Fi."
options.motivation: Using the same excuse repeatedly makes you look "unreliable." It suggests you don't value the "Quality" of the meeting.

question_text: You are working from a popular co-working space or cafe. You need to take a call.
historical_context: The rise of the "Mobile Office" has created new frictions. Middle-class civility requires minimizing your "noise footprint" in shared spaces.

options.text: Stay at your table and speak loudly so everyone can hear your "important" business.
options.motivation: This is "low-status" behavior. It is inconsiderate of the shared "Public Arena" and makes you look like you are "performing" success.

options.text: Use headphones and step into a phone booth or outside to keep the conversation private.
options.motivation: This shows "Social Navigation" awareness. It protects the "Privé en Zakelijk" boundary of your call and respects others' space.

options.text: Don't take the call; just text them back later.
options.motivation: While quiet, it might delay an important "Reliability" moment. Stepping away is the more professional balance.

question_text: A colleague is collecting €5 from everyone for a birthday gift for the manager.
historical_context: Office collections are small "Tithes" to the social harmony of the workplace. The amount matters less than the act of participating as a peer.

options.text: Complain loudly that you don't even like the manager.
options.motivation: This is a "Social Navigation" disaster. It makes you look like a "friction point" in the team's harmony.

options.text: Contribute the €5 gracefully and sign the card with a brief, professional note.
options.motivation: This is "Social Contract" behavior. It shows you are a team player who supports the "Public Arena" traditions of the office.

options.text: Offer to pay €50 to show that you are the most generous person in the office.
options.motivation: This is "opscheppen" (boasting) and can make others feel "onderdanig" (inferior) because they can't afford the same.

question_text: You are in a "brainstorming session" on beanbags or sofas. How do you sit?
historical_context: "Agile" and "Creative" workspaces have blurred the lines of formality. The middle-class professional stays "alert" even when the furniture is "relaxed."

options.text: Lie down completely and close your eyes to "think."
options.motivation: This is too "Private Arena." It signals "disinterest" and a lack of "Professional Mirror."

options.text: Sit upright with an open posture, keeping your feet on the floor.
options.motivation: Even in a casual setting, your "Stance" should project "Interest" and "Expertise." It separates you from a student lounging at home.

options.text: Sit on the floor to show how "edgy" and creative you are.
options.motivation: While creative, it can look "onderdanig" (submissive) compared to others in chairs.

question_text: You realize mid-presentation that a slide has a major typo. Your peers notice.
historical_context: Accountability is the cornerstone of middle-class trust. Admitting a small error with grace prevents it from becoming a large reputation crisis.

options.text: Pretend it isn't there and keep talking faster.
options.motivation: This looks like you are "hiding." It lowers the perceived "Quality" of your work.

options.text: "Ah, I see a typo there—apologies. I'll ensure the corrected 'Quality' version is in your inbox after this."
options.motivation: Acknowledge, apologize briefly, and pivot back to the "Merit" of the content. This maintains your "Reliability."

options.text: Blame the person who gave you the template.
options.motivation: Shifting blame is a "Low Status" move. In the middle class, you are responsible for the "Mirror" you present.

question_text: You are at a professional conference and want to post about it.
historical_context: Self-promotion in the middle class must always be wrapped in "Utility." You aren't posting because you are "great"; you are posting because the information is great.

options.text: Post a "duck-face" selfie with a drink and no caption.
options.motivation: This is for the "Private Arena" (Instagram/TikTok). It confuses your "Professional Brand."

options.text: Post a photo of the keynote speaker or a group shot, with 3 bullet points of what you learned.
options.motivation: This is "Merit-based Posting." It shows you are at the event to gain "Quality" insights, not just to be seen.

options.text: Post nothing because you are too "cool" for LinkedIn.
options.motivation: In the 19-30 age group, having no professional presence can make you look like you lack "initiative" or digital competence.

question_text: You have just purchased your first high-quality car. A colleague at the office notices it in the parking lot and says, "Nice ride, is that new?"
historical_context: Middle-class wealth is "earned." Therefore, etiquette dictates that you justify spending through logic (safety, durability) rather than prestige or raw power.

options.text: "Yes! It’s the [Model Name]. It has every possible upgrade and cost me a lot, but I wanted the best."
options.motivation: Mentioning costs and "wanting the best" as a status marker is seen as "boasting" (opscheppen). It violates the rule of Material Modesty.

options.text: "Thanks! I’m really happy with it. I needed something reliable and safe for the long commute to the office."
options.motivation: This justifies the purchase through "Utility" and "Reliability." You acknowledge the success while keeping the focus on professional necessity.

options.text: "It’s just an old second-hand thing, nothing much."
options.motivation: If the car is clearly new or high-end, this "false modesty" can feel dishonest or like you are "hiding" your success from your peers.

question_text: A group of friends from university, who now work in different sectors, ask you exactly how much your "net" monthly salary is.
historical_context: The taboo on salary prevents direct friction among peers who are all trying to climb the same meritocratic ladder.

options.text: Share the exact number to see how you compare to the market.
options.motivation: While common among very close friends, in a broader professional-social circle, this is a taboo. It can create "financial competition" that ruins social harmony.

options.text: "I’m happy with where I am. It’s a fair reflection of my current responsibilities and the 'Quality' of work I put in."
options.motivation: This maintains modesty by pivoting the conversation from money to merit and responsibility.

options.text: "I make way more than you guys, that’s for sure."
options.motivation: This is a direct violation of middle-class social navigation. It makes you look arrogant and marks you as "Elite-distanced".

question_text: You received a performance bonus. You want to celebrate. How do you handle this in the workplace?
historical_context: In the middle-class matrix, success should be visible through the quality of your life, not the flashiness of your individual purchases.

options.text: Buy everyone in the office lunch and announce it’s because of your bonus.
options.motivation: While generous, announcing why you are paying can be seen as "boasting." It makes those who didn't get a bonus feel "underdanig" (inferior).

options.text: Treat a few close colleagues to coffee or a modest drink without making a big announcement about the money.
options.motivation: This builds "Social Capital" and shares the success without breaking the "Materiële Bescheidenheid" (Material Modesty) code.

options.text: Go to the office the next day with a very expensive designer bag or watch and tell everyone the bonus paid for it.
options.motivation: This is "conspicuous consumption." It signals that you value the "reward" more than the "merit" of the work.

question_text: You took a trip to a high-end resort. A peer asks, "How was your holiday?"
historical_context: For the rising middle class, travel is framed as "Self-Improvement" or "Restoration" for the next period of hard work.

options.text: "It was great! We focused on the 'Experience' and the 'Quality' of the local culture. It was very refreshing."
options.motivation: This uses "Quality" and "Experience" as codewords for success. It shows you value personal growth over luxury.

options.text: "The hotel was so expensive, you wouldn't believe the price of a cocktail there."
options.motivation: Focus on price is seen as unrefined. It suggests you were more interested in the "cost" than the "experience".

options.text: "It was fine, just a standard week away."
options.motivation: If you actually had a great time, being too dismissive can make you seem "afstandelijk" (distant) or ungrateful.

question_text: You need a new laptop bag for work. What is the "Middle Class" choice?
historical_context: "The Hidden Detail"—quality that is felt but not shouted—is the primary marker of the established middle class.

options.text: A bag with a very large, recognizable luxury logo so people know you can afford it.
options.motivation: Visible logos are considered "brash." Refinement in this matrix is about "inconspicuous quality".

options.text: A high-quality leather or durable fabric bag with no visible logo but excellent craftsmanship.
options.motivation: This signals "Reliability" and "Merit" to those who know quality. It shows you care about the "details" without shouting.

options.text: The cheapest plastic bag you can find to show you don't care about material things.
options.motivation: Being "unverzorgd" (unkept) suggests you might not be meticulous in your professional duties either.

question_text: You’ve moved from a student flat to a nice apartment in a good area. How do you describe it to your manager?
historical_context: In Belgium, home and living space are private "Arenas." You share the logic of the move, not the prestige of the address.

options.text: "I finally moved to [Prestigious Area]. The rent is high, but it's where people like us live."
options.motivation: This is "Elite Arrogance." It creates a distance between you and others based on location rather than merit.

options.text: "It’s a very practical space. The 'Quality' of life there is great because I’m closer to the office and it’s very quiet."
options.motivation: This justifies the move through "Utility" and "Professional Efficiency".

options.text: "It's a palace! You should see the view, it's incredible."
options.motivation: Too enthusiastic. In the middle class, one should remain "bescheiden" (modest) about personal luxury.

question_text: You are out with a group of friends who are all at different stages of their careers. You are doing well, but some are struggling.
historical_context: The "Peer-to-Peer" contract of the middle class requires that no one person makes their wealth a source of discomfort for others in the group.

options.text: Order the most expensive bottle of wine for the table and say, "Don't worry, I've got this."
options.motivation: This makes others feel "onderdanig" (inferior). It’s a "power move" that breaks social harmony.

options.text: Follow the lead of the group. If the group is ordering modestly, you do the same to maintain the "Mirror."
options.motivation: This is "Social Navigation." You prioritize the comfort of the group over your own ability to spend.

options.text: Only order water and a side salad to make sure no one thinks you're showing off.
options.motivation: This can make the group feel awkward if they are actually trying to enjoy a nice meal.

question_text: A colleague is getting married. There is a "gift list" or a "money envelope" tradition.
historical_context: Gift-giving is about "Civility" and acknowledging the "Merit" of the relationship, not about the raw dollar value.

options.text: Give a massive amount of money so your name is at the top of the list.
options.motivation: This is "opscheppen" (boasting). It turns a social celebration into a financial competition.

options.text: Give an amount that is "appropriate" for your role and relationship—standard for the "Middle Class" circle.
options.motivation: This shows you understand the "Social Contract." You are being a "Reliable" participant without drawing attention to your wealth.

options.text: Don't give anything because "your presence is the gift."
options.motivation: This is seen as "unverzorgd" (negligent). In the middle class, participation in these rituals is a sign of social reliability.

question_text: You bought the newest, most expensive smartphone. A peer asks about it.
historical_context: Technology is the "New Merit." Owning it is acceptable if it serves your "Professional Brand" and your efficiency.

options.text: "It’s amazing. It has the best camera on the market and was the most expensive one in the shop."
options.motivation: Again, focusing on "most expensive" is the primary taboo. It signals that you value status over utility.

options.text: "I’ve found it really improves my 'Productivity.' The battery life and the 'Quality' of the interface make my work much easier."
options.motivation: Framing a luxury item as a tool for "Merit" and "Efficiency" is the correct middle-class approach.

options.text: "Oh, it's just a phone. I don't really know how it works."
options.motivation: This can seem "dismissive" of the very high-quality item you clearly chose to buy.

question_text: You are successful and want to help a younger sibling or friend by giving them an expensive item you no longer use (like a designer suit or a laptop).
historical_context: Generosity in the middle class is framed as "Resource Sharing" rather than "Charity." It’s about keeping high-quality items in use within the "Arena."

options.text: "Here, take this. You could never afford something this nice on your own."
options.motivation: This is "Elite Distance." It demeans the receiver and highlights their lack of merit compared to yours.

options.text: "I’m upgrading my setup and this is still in 'Excellent Quality.' I’d love for it to get more use if you could find it helpful."
options.motivation: This focuses on the "Quality" of the item and its "Utility." It preserves the dignity of the person receiving the gift.

options.text: Sell it to them at full price to teach them the value of money.
options.motivation: While "merit-based," it might be perceived as "petty" between close friends or family.

question_text: During a creative session, a colleague presents an idea you shared with them privately yesterday as their own. The manager is impressed. How do you react?
historical_context: Professional middle-class ethics rely on "Individual Merit." Reclaiming credit without causing a "scene" is a vital skill for those rising through the corporate ranks.

options.text: Interrupt immediately and say, "Actually, I told you that yesterday, stop stealing my work."
options.motivation: This is too aggressive and emotional. It disrupts the "Public Arena" and makes you look difficult to work with rather than a victim of theft.

options.text: "I’m glad you brought that up, [Name]. When I was developing that concept yesterday, I also thought about [New Detail]. How do you think that fits the 'Quality' of the plan?"
options.motivation: This is "Adaptive Linguistics." You reclaim ownership by providing deeper "Merit" and details only the creator would know, all while staying professional and polite.

options.text: Say nothing and wait until you can talk to the manager alone to complain.
options.motivation: While safe, it can look "underdanig" (submissive). In the middle class, you are expected to (gracefully) stand up for your own professional brand.

question_text: A senior leader with 20 years more experience than you makes a factual error in a report that will be sent to a client.
historical_context: The "Junior-Senior" dynamic in Europe is based on "Respectful Peerage." You respect their experience, but they respect your attention to "Quality."

options.text: Correct them loudly in front of the whole team to show how smart you are.
options.motivation: This is a "Social Navigation" disaster. It shames the senior and makes you look like a "know-it-all" with no respect for the "Arena".

options.text: Send a private, polite message: "I was reviewing the report to ensure our 'Quality' standards; I noticed a small discrepancy in the data on page 4. Should we adjust that before it goes out?"
options.motivation: This protects the "Professional Mirror" of the senior while framing the correction as a service to the "Reliability" of the firm.

options.text: Just fix it yourself and don't tell them.
options.motivation: If they find out later, they may feel you are "going behind their back." Transparency is part of the "Middle Class" social contract.

question_text: You asked for a promotion or a lead role, but you were told "not yet." How do you handle the exit from that meeting?
historical_context: In the middle class, a career is a "Long Game." Handling rejection with grace is a marker of emotional maturity and high-level social navigation.

options.text: Ask for specific "Merit-based" feedback: "I understand. What specific 'Quality' markers or 'Reliability' goals should I hit over the next six months to be ready?"
options.motivation: This shows a "Growth Mindset." You stay professional and turn a "No" into a roadmap for future success.

options.text: Tell them that you’ll start looking for a new job immediately since they don't value you.
options.motivation: This is an emotional ultimatum. It destroys your "Reliability" and makes the manager glad they didn't promote you.

options.text: Shrug and say "That’s fine," then do the bare minimum work for the rest of the month.
options.motivation: This "Passive-Aggressive" behavior is a major taboo. It signals that your work is "transactional" rather than "merit-based."

question_text: You arrive at a client event and realize you are significantly under-dressed (e.g., they are in suits, you are in a polo).
historical_context: In modern "Public Arenas," the person who can stay calm despite a wardrobe error is perceived as having high "Social Status" and self-assurance.

options.text: Leave immediately before anyone sees you.
options.motivation: This is "Unreliable" behavior. You are missing the "Arena" moment because of a small "Mirror" error.

options.text: Own it with a brief smile: "I seem to have interpreted the 'Casual' in 'Business Casual' a bit too literally! Regardless, I'm excited to discuss the 'Quality' of our new project."
options.motivation: You acknowledge the error briefly (Social Navigation) and then pivot immediately back to "Merit." It shows high confidence.

options.text: Spend the whole night apologizing to everyone for your clothes.
options.motivation: This makes you look "onderdanig" (submissive). It draws more attention to the mistake and away from your professional value.

question_text: A group of peers is gossiping about a colleague’s "Private Arena" (e.g., a divorce or personal trouble). They ask for your opinion.
historical_context: Discretion is the hallmark of the "Refined Middle Class." The ability to separate a person’s work merit from their private life is a foundational etiquette rule.

options.text: "I prefer to focus on our 'Professional Mirror' here. [Name] always delivers high 'Quality' work, so I’m sure they’ll handle their personal things fine."
options.motivation: This reinforces the "Privé en Zakelijk" (Private/Business) boundary and protects the "Reliability" of the team member.

options.text: Listen to all the details but don't say anything yourself.
options.motivation: You are still participating in the "Low Status" behavior of gossiping. It subtly lowers your own professional brand.

options.text: Join in and share a secret you heard about that person too.
options.motivation: This is a total failure of "Social Navigation." It marks you as "unreliable" and untrustworthy.

question_text: You realize a high-priority project will not be finished by the deadline.
historical_context: In professional services, "Due Diligence" and "Quality Control" are more important than rushing, provided you communicate early.

options.text: Wait until the deadline passes and then apologize.
