options.motivation: This is the ultimate "Unreliable" move. It destroys trust and suggests a lack of planning.

options.text: Contact the stakeholder 48 hours early: "To ensure the 'Quality' of the final deliverable meets our standards, I need an extra 24 hours. I want to guarantee total 'Reliability' on the data."
options.motivation: You frame the delay as a choice to protect "Quality." It turns a failure into a "Reliability" check.

options.text: Work 20 hours straight to finish it, even if the work is full of errors.
options.motivation: Producing "Low Quality" work to meet a deadline is often worse than asking for a brief extension in the middle-class matrix.

question_text: A manager gives you feedback that feels personal and overly harsh during a one-on-one.
historical_context: Professionalism acts as a "buffer." The middle-class etiquette requires keeping the "Work" and the "Person" separate during feedback loops.

options.text: "I appreciate you're looking for 'Quality.' Let's focus on the specific 'Merit' markers I can improve. Can we leave the personal comments aside?"
options.motivation: This is "Adaptive Linguistics." You firmly redirect the conversation back to the "Professional Arena" without being rude.

options.text: Start crying and say you're doing your best.
options.motivation: While a natural reaction, in the "Public Arena," it can make you look like you aren't ready for high-stakes responsibility.

options.text: Argue back and tell them they are a bad manager.
options.motivation: This destroys the "Professional Mirror" and creates a conflict you likely cannot win as a junior.

question_text: A competitor at a trade show makes a negative comment about your company’s "Reliability."
historical_context: In the "Public Arena," the winner is often the one who remains the most "verzorgd" (polished) and calm under attack.

options.text: "We prefer to let our 'Quality' and 'Merit' speak for itself. Our long-term client retention is what we focus on."
options.motivation: This is "The High Road." You don't sink to their level; you use your "Professional Brand" as a shield.

options.text: Tell them that their company is even worse.
options.motivation: This makes both of you look "low status" and unrefined. It creates a "dirty" Arena.

options.text: Walk away without saying anything.
options.motivation: Silence can sometimes look like an admission of guilt. A brief, polite defense of your "Merit" is usually better.

question_text: A friend tags you in a controversial or highly political post while you are at work.
historical_context: The digital age has blurred the lines of the "Social Contract." The middle class must work harder to keep their "Public" and "Private" faces separate.

options.text: Leave it up; you believe in freedom of speech.
options.motivation: While true, your "Professional Mirror" is now linked to a controversy. This can make you look "unreliable" to clients or bosses.

options.text: Untag yourself immediately and send a private note: "I love our debates, but let's keep them in the 'Private Arena' so my 'Professional Brand' stays neutral."
options.motivation: This manages the "Privé en Zakelijk" boundary effectively.

options.text: Comment on the post and start a heated argument with strangers.
options.motivation: This is a disaster for "Social Navigation." It shows a lack of emotional control in a "Public Arena."

question_text: A client asks you to "bend the rules" or hide a small piece of data to make them look better.
historical_context: The "Middle Class" was founded on the idea of "Fair Play" and "Trust." Ethical reliability is the ultimate currency in this social matrix.

options.text: "I can't do that. My 'Reliability' and the 'Quality' of my work are based on total transparency. Let's find a way to present the truth effectively."
options.motivation: This is "Merit-based Integrity." You refuse the request but offer a professional solution instead.

options.text: Do it just this once because you don't want to lose the client.
options.motivation: Once you compromise your "Merit," you can never get it back. You become "unreliable" by definition.

options.text: Report the client to the police immediately.
options.motivation: Unless it's a major crime, this is an overreaction that destroys the "Social Navigation" of the relationship. Try to redirect first.

question_text: You accidentally post a private, slightly complain-y video about a difficult client to your "Close Friends" story, but a "friend" leaks it to the client.
historical_context: With the collapse of the boundary between "Private and Business" due to social media, the middle class has had to adopt a "Politician’s Discipline" regarding their digital footprint.

options.text: Delete the video, block everyone, and deny that it was you in the post.
options.motivation: This is "Unreliable." In the digital age, denials of clear evidence destroy your "Professional Brand" faster than the original mistake.

options.text: Call the client immediately: "I am deeply sorry. That video was a lapse in judgment and does not reflect the 'Quality' of respect I have for our partnership. I take full responsibility."
options.motivation: This is "Radical Accountability." By owning the error before it spreads further, you protect your "Reliability" and show the maturity of a true professional.

options.text: Post a public apology on LinkedIn explaining how stressed you’ve been lately.
options.motivation: This brings the crisis into the broad "Public Arena" unnecessarily. It’s better to handle the specific "Mirror" of the relationship privately first.

question_text: You were the lead on a junior project that missed a critical deadline. Your manager is looking for an explanation in front of the board.
historical_context: Middle-class leadership is built on "Service and Accountability." A leader is a shield for their team, taking the blame in public and coaching in private.

options.text: "I did my part, but the other two juniors on the team didn't hit their 'Quality' targets on time."
options.motivation: Throwing your peers under the bus is "Low Status." It signals that you are not ready for leadership roles in the middle-class matrix.

options.text: "As the lead, the 'Reliability' of this output rests with me. We faced [X] challenge, and I am already implementing a 'Quality' check process to ensure this never happens again."
options.motivation: You claim the failure. This shows the manager you have the "Authority" and "Merit" to lead, even in a crisis.

options.text: Stay silent and hope the manager doesn't ask you specifically.
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig" (submissive). A rising professional must be visible and accountable to be taken seriously.

question_text: You find evidence that a senior colleague is taking "kickbacks" (bribes) from a vendor. You are 24 and they are 50.
historical_context: The middle class was founded on "Fair Play." Corruption is seen as an "Elite" or "Underclass" vice; the middle class maintains its status through adherence to the law.

options.text: Keep quiet; they are powerful and you are just starting your career.
options.motivation: While safe in the short term, if the scandal breaks later, your "Reliability" and "Merit" will be questioned because you knew.

options.text: Document the evidence and follow the formal "Quality" protocol (HR or Compliance). Frame it as protecting the company’s "Professional Brand."
options.motivation: This is "Merit-based Integrity." You are following the "Social Contract" of the middle class: the rules apply to everyone regardless of age or rank.

options.text: Try to blackmail the senior colleague for a promotion.
options.motivation: This is a total loss of "Professional Branding." It moves you from "Rising Professional" to "Criminal," ending your career.

question_text: Your mental health is suffering, and your "Quality" of work has noticeably dropped. Your boss asks for a "chat."
historical_context: Modern middle-class etiquette treats "Burnout" as a technical maintenance issue—like a machine needing service—rather than a moral failing.

options.text: Tell them everything about your personal life and cry in the office.
options.motivation: This blurs the "Privé en Zakelijk" boundary too much. It can make you look "unreliable" for future high-pressure tasks.

options.text: "I’ve noticed the drop in my 'Quality' too. I’m currently managing some personal matters and I’d like to discuss a temporary 'Reliability' plan to ensure the work stays on track."
options.motivation: This is "Adaptive Linguistics." You acknowledge the problem but focus on the "Solution" and the "Work," which preserves your professional brand.

options.text: Deny anything is wrong and say you are doing your best.
options.motivation: This is "gaslighting" the data. If the quality is down, denying it makes you look like you lack self-awareness or "Merit."

question_text: A client posts a scathing, public review of your work on Google or LinkedIn, but their facts are completely wrong.
historical_context: Reputation management in the middle class is about "Coolness under fire." The person who stays calm wins the audience.

options.text: Respond with an angry list of all the things the client did wrong.
options.motivation: This is a "Public Arena" disaster. It makes you look defensive and "low status." You lose the "Professional Mirror."

options.text: "Thank you for the feedback. We strive for the highest 'Quality.' I will reach out privately to resolve these discrepancies and ensure our 'Reliability' remains intact."
options.motivation: This is "Social Navigation." You show the public that you are the "Adult in the room," moving the conflict to a private space immediately.

options.text: Ignore it and hope it goes away.
options.motivation: In the digital age, silence can look like an admission of poor quality. A brief, polished response is necessary.

question_text: A close friend at work has become lazy, and it’s affecting your "Merit" because you share a project.
historical_context: The "Professional Peer" dynamic requires that friends hold each other to the same "Merit" standards to which they hold themselves.

options.text: Stop being friends with them and tell everyone they are lazy.
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd" (unpolished) social behavior. It creates a toxic "Arena" and makes you look like a poor teammate.

options.text: "As a friend, I’m worried about you. As a colleague, I’m worried about the 'Quality' of our project. Let’s get back on track so our 'Reliability' isn't questioned."
options.motivation: You use the "Professional Mirror" to protect the friendship. You frame the work as a shared "Asset" that both must protect.

options.text: Do all their work for them so the boss doesn't find out.
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig" (submissive). It doesn't solve the "Reliability" issue and eventually leads to your own burnout.

question_text: You are hosting a small group at a restaurant. The waiter is incredibly rude to one of your guests.
historical_context: The "Middle Class Host" is the guardian of the "Public Arena." Their job is to maintain harmony through subtle power, not brute force.

options.text: Start a loud argument with the waiter to "defend" your friend.
options.motivation: This is "low-status" behavior. It ruins the "Social Arena" for your guest and makes you look out of control.

options.text: Quietly signal the manager or wait until the end to say: "We value the 'Quality' of this venue, but the service tonight didn't match your usual 'Reliability'."
options.motivation: This is "Social Navigation." You protect your guest's comfort without creating a scene, and you provide "merit-based" feedback.

options.text: Do nothing because you are embarrassed.
options.motivation: As the "Host," you have the "Authority" to ensure the guest's comfort. Failing to act shows a lack of social maturity.

question_text: The company is downscaling, and you are being let go through no fault of your own. How do you leave?
historical_context: In a meritocratic economy, "Career Mobility" is expected. Leaving a job with grace is the final act of "Reliability" for that employer.

options.text: Steal company files and badmouth the boss on the way out.
options.motivation: This destroys your "Professional Brand" for the rest of your life. The "Arena" is smaller than you think.

options.text: "I’ve enjoyed contributing to the 'Quality' of this team. I’m looking forward to bringing my 'Reliability' to my next role. How can we make this transition smooth?"
options.motivation: This is "The High Road." You leave as a high-merit professional, ensuring your references stay perfect and your reputation intact.

options.text: Cry in the lobby and refuse to leave your desk.
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig" (submissive). It lacks the "Professional Mirror" expected of a rising middle-class peer.

question_text: You made a decision (like a budget cut) that is very unpopular with your peers (aged 19-30).
historical_context: Leadership in the middle class often requires "Self-Sacrifice" for the sake of the "Social Contract."

options.text: "I didn't want to do it, the 'Elites' at the top forced me."
options.motivation: This shows you have no "Authority." It makes you look like a tool of the "Elites" rather than a merit-based leader.

options.text: "I understand this is difficult. This decision was made to ensure the long-term 'Reliability' of our department and protect the 'Quality' of our core work."
options.motivation: You frame the "Unpopular" move as a "Protective" move. It shows you value the "Merit" of the organization over temporary popularity.

options.text: Tell everyone to "deal with it" and walk away.
options.motivation: Too aggressive. It fails in "Social Navigation" and creates a "dirty" Arena for future collaboration.

question_text: A false rumor about your "Private Arena" is circulating in the office.
historical_context: "Character" in the middle class is a slow-build asset. A single rumor cannot destroy a "Reliable" brand built on years of consistent "Quality."

options.text: Post a long, emotional defense on the company Slack/Teams channel.
options.motivation: This gives the rumor more "Airtime" and blurs the "Privé en Zakelijk" boundary in a messy way.

options.text: Ignore the rumor but increase the "Quality" and "Visibility" of your work. Let your "Merit" be the answer to the gossip.
options.motivation: This is the "Professional Mirror" approach. In the middle class, "Results" are the ultimate defense against character attacks.

options.text: Find out who started it and confront them in the parking lot.
options.motivation: Too aggressive. It moves the conflict from the "Public Arena" to a "Low Status" physical confrontation.

question_text: You are being introduced to a new male project lead. How do you establish an immediate "Professional Mirror" of reliability?
historical_context: The handshake evolved from a gesture to show one was not carrying a weapon. In the modern middle class, it is a "Calibration Tool" used to measure a man's confidence and social awareness.

options.text: Give a very firm, almost painful "bone-crusher" grip to show you are the alpha.
options.motivation: This is seen as "brash" and insecure. In the middle-class matrix, physical aggression—even in a handshake—is unrefined and breaks social harmony.

options.text: A firm (but not squeezing) grip, three seconds of direct eye contact, and a clear "Pleasure to meet you, [Name]."
options.motivation: This projects "Status through Competence." It shows you are a reliable peer who is neither submissive (onderdanig) nor aggressive.

options.text: A limp, soft handshake while looking at the floor to show you are humble.
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig" (submissive). It fails to project the "Reliability" and expertise expected of a rising professional.

question_text: You are starting a new role in a "Smart Casual" office environment. You prefer having facial hair. What is the standard?
historical_context: In the 19th-century middle class, facial hair was highly regulated by fashion. Today, the rule isn't "shaved vs. beard," but "managed vs. unmanaged."

options.text: Let it grow naturally; a "mountain man" look shows you are authentic.
options.motivation: This looks "unverzorgd" (unkept). It suggests that your attention to detail in your work might be as sloppy as your grooming.

options.text: Keep it neatly trimmed with clean lines on the cheeks and neck, or stay clean-shaven.
options.motivation: This signals "Reliability." It shows that you put effort into your "Professional Mirror" and understand that details matter in the "Public Arena."

options.text: Wear a "designer stubble" but don't shave your neck to save time.
options.motivation: The "unshaven neck" is a common error for young men. It can make "Smart Casual" look like "Just Rolled Out of Bed," damaging your brand.

question_text: You are the first to arrive in a meeting room with a large table. Where and how do you sit?
historical_context: Occupying space is a traditional marker of status. Middle-class etiquette requires occupying space "Efficiently"—showing presence without infringing on others.

options.text: Sit at the very head of the table to show you are a future leader.
options.motivation: If you are the most junior person, this can look like "Elite Arrogance." It’s better to leave the power seats for the facilitators.

options.text: Sit in a middle seat, sit upright with your hands on the table, and keep your legs parallel or slightly crossed at the ankle.
options.motivation: This "Stance" projects "Active Interest" and "Expertise." It avoids the "spaced-out" look of someone who doesn't belong.

options.text: Slouch low in the chair with your legs spread wide (manspreading).
options.motivation: This is an aggressive use of space that is seen as unrefined and disrespectful to the shared "Public Arena."

question_text: A female colleague makes a minor technical mistake during her presentation. You are the only one who noticed.
historical_context: The concept of the "Gentleman" in the middle class shifted from "Protector" to "Professional Peer." The goal is mutual success through civility.

options.text: Shout out the correction immediately so she doesn't keep saying the wrong thing.
options.motivation: This is "brash" and ruins the "Social Navigation" of her presentation. It makes you look like you are trying to diminish her merit.

options.text: Wait until a break or send a quick, private "quality check" message: "Great points! I noticed a small typo on slide 3; just wanted to flag it before the final version goes out."
options.motivation: This is "Peer-based Respect." You protect her "Professional Mirror" while ensuring the "Quality" of the work is maintained.

options.text: Say nothing and tell your boss later that she doesn't know what she's talking about.
options.motivation: This is "underhanded" and violates the middle-class social contract of teamwork and transparency.

question_text: You are going to a high-stakes client meeting. How do you handle cologne?
historical_context: In the 18th century, perfume was used to hide lack of hygiene. In the modern middle class, hygiene is assumed, so fragrance is a "Hidden Detail" only for those very close to you.

options.text: Use 5-6 sprays of a famous luxury scent so they know you have "Expensive" taste.
options.motivation: Overpowering scents are a major taboo. It is seen as "try-hard" and inconsiderate of the shared air in the "Arena."

options.text: One or two sprays maximum, or no scent at all. Your "Professional Brand" should be "Clean and Neutral."
options.motivation: "Limited accessories and scents" is the rule. You want them to remember your "Merit," not your smell.

options.text: Use a cheap body spray from the gym locker room.
options.motivation: These scents are often too synthetic and "juvenile." They can lower your perceived age and expertise level.

question_text: You are walking toward a door with a group of male and female colleagues.
historical_context: Chivalry (protecting women) has evolved into Civility (respecting everyone). In the middle class, the "Host" or "First Arrived" facilitates the movement of the group.

options.text: Rush to the front, open the door, and bow for every woman to pass through.
options.motivation: This can feel archaic or "over-the-top" in a modern merit-based office. It can sometimes emphasize gender over professional peerage.

options.text: If you are at the door first, hold it open for whoever is behind you (male or female), make eye contact, and follow them in.
options.motivation: This is "Public Arena" civility. You show awareness and respect for the group without making it a "gender performance."

options.text: Walk through and let the door swing shut behind you.
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd" (uncaring) behavior. It shows a lack of social awareness and lowers your "Reliability."

question_text: A male peer is speaking to you in an aggressive, loud tone during a disagreement.
historical_context: Emotional regulation is a primary marker of the "Educated Middle Class." Losing one's temper was historically seen as a loss of "Gentlemanly" status.

options.text: Match his volume and get even louder to show you won't be bullied.
options.motivation: This is a descent into "Low Status" conflict. It destroys the "Professional Mirror" for both of you.

options.text: Lower your own volume slightly and speak at a slower pace: "I understand you're frustrated. Let's look at the 'Quality' of the data so we can find a 'Reliable' solution."
options.motivation: This is "Adaptive Linguistics." By staying calm, you assert "Authority" and force the other person to match your professional level.

options.text: Walk away without saying anything and block them on all platforms.
options.motivation: This is "Passive." In the middle class, you are expected to navigate conflict with words, not avoidance.

question_text: You want to buy a watch to celebrate your first promotion. What is the "Middle Class" choice for a man aged 19-30?
historical_context: The wristwatch moved from the battlefield to the boardroom. It remains the "silent signal" of a man who respects the "Social Contract" of punctuality.

options.text: A massive, gold-colored watch with fake diamonds.
options.motivation: This is "opscheppen" (boasting). It looks "nouveau riche" and violates "Materiële Bescheidenheid."

options.text: A classic steel-case watch with a leather or metal strap—simple, high-quality, and functional.
options.motivation: This is "Professional Branding." It signals that you value "Quality" and "Durability" over "Flash."

options.text: Use your smartphone for the time; watches are for old people.
options.motivation: While practical, a watch is one of the few accepted "Professional Mirrors" for men. It subtly signals that you value time and tradition.

question_text: You are "with the guys" at a work-related social event (a borrel). The conversation is relaxed.
historical_context: "Linguistic Temperance" (controlling your tongue) has been a middle-class virtue for centuries, separating the professional from the "unruly."

options.text: Use as much profanity as you want to show you are "one of the boys."
options.motivation: This is a "Social Navigation" trap. Even in relaxed settings, the "Public Arena" rules apply. Excessive swearing makes you look unrefined.

options.text: Keep your language clean but relaxed. You can be "Informal" without being "Vulgar."
options.motivation: This protects your "Professional Brand." It shows you can be "approachable" without losing your "Expert" status.

options.text: Speak in perfect, stiff formal English to show you are superior.
options.motivation: This is "Elite-distanced." It makes you look like you don't know how to "mirror" the social energy of the group.

question_text: You are walking down a narrow hallway and a senior executive is walking toward you.
historical_context: Hallway etiquette is the "dance" of the Public Arena. Middle-class men are expected to be "Solid" (not pushed around) but "Civil" (not pushing others).

options.text: Stop, flatten yourself against the wall, and bow your head.
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig" (submissive). It suggests you don't feel you belong in the same "Arena."

options.text: Maintain your path but turn your shoulders slightly to give them easy passage, make eye contact, and give a brief "Good morning."
options.motivation: This is "Social Navigation." You acknowledge their "Authority" while maintaining your own "Stance" as a confident peer.

options.text: Walk straight through them and make them move out of your way.
options.motivation: This is "Elite Arrogance" or "Brashness." It creates unnecessary friction in the professional environment.

question_text: You arrive at a local sports club or neighborhood gathering. You see three people talking in a circle. You want to be part of the group without feeling like an intruder.

options.text: Walk in and stand right in the middle, loudly greeting everyone.
options.motivation: This is seen as "forced." By breaking the circle too aggressively, you create a subconscious "threat." People won't like you; they will just want to get away from your energy.

options.text: Stand at the edge, wait for a natural pause and eye contact, then nod and say: "Mind if I join the conversation?"
options.motivation: This is the Likeability Factor. You respect their space first, which makes them feel safe. By asking permission, you show you are a "dignified citizen" who values others, making them want to include you.

options.text: Stand silently behind them and wait to be noticed.
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig" (submissive). It makes you look like you lack the confidence to be a peer. You become the person people ignore, not the person they want to work with.

question_text: You are walking into a new housing development where you just moved. You see a neighbor washing his car. You don’t know him yet.

options.text: Look at your phone and walk past quickly so you don't bother him.
options.motivation: This makes you the "arrogant" new neighbor. In a community, silence is often interpreted as looking down on others. You are closing doors before they even open.

options.text: Give a clear nod, a smile, and a firm "Good morning!" as you pass.
options.motivation: This is the Belgian Superpower. It is the ultimate door-opener. It signals that you are friendly but not "pushy." You are establishing yourself as a reliable, likable neighbor who is "one of us."

options.text: Stop and start asking him how much his car cost and how much he paid for his house.
options.motivation: This is too familiar, too fast. It’s intrusive. You’ll be labeled as the "neighborhood gossip" rather than a dignified peer.

question_text: You are out for a "Smart Casual" lunch with two colleagues. The bill is placed in the center of the table.

options.text: Immediately grab the bill and say: "I've got this, don't worry about it."
options.motivation: This is Commanding Respect. Taking the lead (if it fits the context) shows you are a man of action and success. It makes people feel taken care of, and they will subconsciously want to return the favor later.

options.text: Wait for the oldest person at the table to reach for it.
options.motivation: Safe, but you remain "invisible." You aren't opening any doors by waiting for others to lead.

options.text: Start a long debate about who had the extra coffee and calculate the cents.
options.motivation: This kills the "Likeability Factor." It makes you look petty. No one wants to work with the man who fights over small change; they want to work with the man who sees the "big picture."

question_text: You are talking to a contractor about a renovation project. You want him to prioritize your house over others.

options.text: Lean against the wall, hands in pockets, looking bored.
options.motivation: This signals "disinterest." The contractor will think you don't care about the details, so he won't care about the quality of the work.

options.text: Stand with your feet apart, hands visible, and look him in the eye while he speaks.
options.motivation: This projects Expertise. You look like a man who knows what he wants but respects the craftsman. Contractors work harder for people they respect and who show "Active Interest" in the job.

options.text: Stand very close to him and keep touching his arm to be "friendly."
options.motivation: This is too forced. It feels desperate. You lose your "dignity" by trying too hard to be liked.

question_text: You need a permit, and the person behind the desk is being difficult.

options.text: Get angry and demand to see their boss.
options.motivation: This closes the door permanently. You become "the problem customer." In the middle-class matrix, aggression is a sign that you’ve lost control.

options.text: Use "Adaptive Linguistics": "I can see you're very busy today. I really value your 'Expertise' here—what would you advise is the best way to get this 'Quality' result?"
options.motivation: This is the Likeability Factor at work. By complimenting their expertise and asking for "advice," you turn an enemy into a mentor. They want to help the dignified young man who respects their role.

options.text: Just keep apologizing for being there.
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig" (submissive). They will treat you like a child and put your file at the bottom of the pile.

question_text: You are in a WhatsApp group for your local sports team or neighborhood. Someone posts a slightly annoying complaint.

options.text: Fire back an angry reply immediately to "win" the argument.
options.motivation: You might win the argument, but you lose the "Arena." Everyone else in the group will see you as a "troublemaker." You’ve just closed ten doors at once.

options.text: Wait, then post a calm, helpful solution or a brief "Let’s discuss this at the next meeting."
options.motivation: This is Commanding Respect. You are the "Adult in the room." You look reliable and balanced. You are the man people will call when they need a leader.

options.text: Post a bunch of laughing emojis to mock the person.
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd" (unpolished). It’s juvenile and kills your dignity.

question_text: You are going to a birthday party at a colleague’s home. You aren't sure how formal it is.

options.text: Wear a full suit just to be safe.
options.motivation: You look "arrogant" or "stiff." You make the host feel like their home isn't good enough for your high standards.

options.text: Wear "The Middle Class Uniform": Clean chinos, a crisp shirt (no tie), and polished shoes.
options.motivation: This is the Likeability Factor. You look "verzorgd" (neat) but approachable. You mirror the energy of a successful peer who is ready to have a good time.

options.text: Wear your old gym clothes because "it’s just a house party."
options.motivation: This shows a lack of respect for the host. It says: "I didn't think your party was worth the effort of getting dressed."

question_text: You are at a "Borrel" (drinks) and a senior professional is telling a long story.

options.text: Interrupt him to tell a better story about yourself.
options.motivation: You think you are showing success, but you are actually showing insecurity. People who "opscheppen" (boast) are rarely liked.

options.text: Listen intently, ask one smart follow-up question, and say: "That’s a fascinating insight into the 'Quality' of your industry."
options.motivation: This is Opening Doors. People love to talk about themselves. By being a "High-Quality Listener," you become the most likable man in the room without saying much.

options.text: Look around the room for someone "more important" while he is talking.
options.motivation: This is "afstandelijk" (distanced). It’s a social error that people never forget.

question_text: You are walking into a large office building. There is a receptionist and a security guard.

options.text: Walk past them without looking, acting like you own the place.
options.motivation: This is "Elite Arrogance." You are making enemies of the "Gatekeepers."

options.text: Make eye contact, give a warm "Good morning," and state your name and who you are meeting.
options.motivation: This is Social Intelligence. Gatekeepers (receptionists, assistants) control the flow of the building. If they like you, your path is twice as easy.

options.text: Mumble your name while looking at the floor.
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig" (submissive). It suggests you aren't sure if you belong there.

question_text: The party or networking event is winding down. How do you leave?

options.text: Sneak out the back door so you don't have to say goodbye.
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd" (unpolished). You leave a "void" where your reputation should be.

options.text: Find the host, look them in the eye, and say: "Thank you for the 'Quality' evening. It was a pleasure to be here."
options.motivation: This is the Lasting Impression. You end the night on a high note of "Dignity." People will remember you as the likable man who showed them respect.

options.text: Stay until the very end and help them clean up without being asked.
options.motivation: While helpful, it can sometimes blur the "Privé en Zakelijk" boundary if you aren't close friends. It can look a bit "forced."

question_text: You’ve bought a high-quality watch with your first big commission. A friend asks, "Is that a new watch? Must have cost a fortune!"

options.text: "Yeah, it’s a [Brand]. It’s an investment in my 'Professional Brand'—I only buy the best."
options.motivation: This kills the Likeability Factor. It sounds like you are reading from a textbook rather than being a dignified peer. People will think you are "opscheppen" (boasting) about your status.

options.text: "Thanks! I’ve always appreciated the 'Quality' and the history of this brand. It’s built to last a lifetime, which I value."
options.motivation: This is the Secret of Quality. You justify the item through its "Durability" and "Craftsmanship." You aren't showing off your money; you are showing off your good taste and long-term thinking.

options.text: "Oh, it’s just a cheap replica, I’d never spend real money on this."
options.motivation: This is "False Modesty." If people find out it's real, they will think you are dishonest. A dignified citizen owns his choices without shouting about them.

question_text: You’ve just moved out of a shared flat into a nice apartment. At a neighborhood BBQ, someone asks about your new place.

options.text: "It’s a great 'Public Arena' for me. The square footage is much higher than my last place."
options.motivation: Too clinical. You sound like a real estate agent, not a neighbor. It lacks the warmth that builds likeability.

options.text: "It’s a very practical space. I really value the quiet neighborhood and the 'Quality' of the light in the mornings. It helps me stay focused."
options.motivation: This is Dignified Citizenship. You are talking about "Quality of Life" and "Utility." It makes you relatable—everyone wants a quiet home and a good start to the day.

options.text: "It’s a massive upgrade. I’m finally living where the successful people live."
options.motivation: This is "Elite Arrogance." It implies that your previous neighbors (or the people you are talking to) aren't successful. You’re closing doors with your tongue.

question_text: You’ve had a great month and want to treat your group of friends (who earn less than you) to dinner.

options.text: Order the most expensive champagne and say, "Drink up, guys, I’m rich today!"
options.motivation: This makes your friends feel "onderdanig" (submissive). You are turned from a "friend" into a "benefactor." It creates a debt that kills the joy of the evening.

options.text: Pick a high-quality, local bistro and say, "I’ve had a good run lately and wanted to share the 'Quality' time with you guys. This one's on me."
options.motivation: This is the Likeability Factor. You acknowledge your success briefly but focus on the "Sharing" and the "Quality" of the friendship. You are a leader who brings others up with him.

options.text: Don't offer to pay at all, even though you invited them, because "everyone should pay their own merit."
options.motivation: This is "stiff." While technically fair, a dignified citizen knows when to be generous to open the hearts of those around him.

question_text: A younger cousin asks you, "How much do you actually make? I want to know if my degree is worth it."

options.text: Tell him the exact "netto" amount and show him your bank app.
options.motivation: This breaks the "Middle Class" taboo. It can lead to jealousy or unrealistic expectations. It lacks the "Refinement" of a professional.

options.text: "I’m at a level where the 'Reliability' of my work is well-rewarded. It allows for a good 'Quality' of life, but the real value is the expertise I’m building."
options.motivation: This is Commanding Respect. You show that money is a result of merit, not the goal itself. You inspire him to focus on his "Quality" of work rather than just the paycheck.

options.text: "I don't talk about money with people like you."
options.motivation: This is "Elite Distance." It makes you look like a cold aristocrat. You lose the chance to be a mentor and a likable relative.

question_text: You are upgrading your laptop or professional tools. You want to give your old (but still high-quality) one to a younger friend.

options.text: "I’m getting the new one because this one is too slow for my high-level work. You can have it."
options.motivation: This emphasizes your "Superior Merit" over theirs. It can make the gift feel like a "handout" rather than a gesture of peerage.

options.text: "I’m switching setups, but this machine still has excellent 'Quality' and 'Reliability.' I’d love for it to go to someone who will put it to good use."
options.motivation: This is Opening Doors. You are passing on a tool of "Merit." You focus on the tool’s quality, which preserves the dignity of the person receiving it.

options.text: Leave it in the trash and tell them where to find it so it's not "awkward."
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd." It shows a lack of respect for the item and the person. A dignified citizen is direct and respectful in his generosity.

question_text: A senior neighbor or older colleague is explaining a process to you, but they are clearly using outdated information that will cause a problem.

options.text: Interrupt them and say: "That’s not how we do things anymore; you’re living in the past."
options.motivation: This is "brash." You might be right, but you’ve just closed the door to that person’s heart. They will see you as arrogant, and you lose the Likeability Factor.

options.text: "I see the logic in that approach. Interestingly, I've seen 'Quality' results lately using [New Method]. What would you advise is the best way to bridge those two ideas?"
options.motivation: This is Opening Doors. You validate their experience first (Dignity), then offer the new "Quality" data as an addition rather than a correction. By asking for "advice," you keep them as an ally.

options.text: Say nothing, let them finish, and then do it your way behind their back.
options.motivation: This is "unreliable." In the middle-class matrix, transparency is key. If the old way fails and you knew better, your "Merit" takes a hit for not speaking up.

question_text: You are at the sports club or a community meeting, and someone blames you for a mistake you didn't make.

options.text: Get angry and start a "shouting match" to defend your honor.
options.motivation: This is "Low Status" behavior. The moment you lose your temper, the "Public Arena" sees you as the problem, not the other person. You lose your Commanding Respect power.

options.text: Maintain a "Solid Stance," look them in the eye, and say calmly: "I understand there’s a frustration here. Let’s look at the facts together so we can ensure the 'Reliability' of the solution."
options.motivation: This is the Likeability Factor through strength. By staying calm while they are emotional, you automatically look like the leader. You move the conflict from "personal" to "merit-based."

options.text: Walk away and cry so they feel bad for you.
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig" (submissive). It suggests you aren't ready to handle the responsibilities of a "Dignified Citizen."

question_text: A friend asks to borrow a significant amount of money that you aren't comfortable lending.

options.text: "I can't lend you the cash, but I’d love to help you look at the 'Quality' of your budget or help you find a different solution. I value our friendship too much to let money get in the middle."
options.motivation: This is Dignified Citizenship. You set a clear boundary (Reliability) but offer a different kind of help (Merit). You protect the relationship while keeping your own "Professional Brand" as a sensible man.

options.text: Lie and say you are completely broke.
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd" (unpolished). If they see you buying something nice later, the trust is broken forever.

options.text: Give them the money even though you know you’ll never see it again.
options.motivation: This is "submissive." You are sacrificing your own "Reliability" for temporary peace. A dignified man makes decisions based on logic, not guilt.

question_text: You promised to help a neighbor with a task at 10:00 AM, but you completely forgot and show up at 2:00 PM.

options.text: Make up a complex lie about your car breaking down.
options.motivation: People can usually smell a lie. It makes you look "unreliable."

options.text: "I completely missed the mark on my timing today. I apologize for the lack of 'Reliability.' I’m here now—how can I provide the best 'Quality' help for the rest of the afternoon?"
options.motivation: This is Commanding Respect. By owning the mistake immediately and focusing on the "Quality" of the work now, you restore your brand. A man who apologizes without making excuses is rare and highly liked.

options.text: Don't apologize; just start working and hope they don't mention it.
options.motivation: This is "arrogant." It shows you don't respect the other person's time. You’ve just closed a door on future favors.

question_text: At a local event, someone makes a joke at your expense to make themselves look "alpha."

options.text: Insult them back with something even meaner.
options.motivation: You have just descended into the "Underclass" conflict style. The "Public Arena" now sees two "unpolished" men arguing.

options.text: Give a short, dry laugh, maintain eye contact, and say: "Interesting point. Now, as we were saying about [Original Topic]..."
options.motivation: This is the Likeability Factor. By not being bothered, you show that their "attack" has no power over you. You look like the "Dignified Notable" who is above petty games.

options.text: Leave the event immediately so you don't have to face them.
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig." You are letting someone else control your "Arena."

question_text: The conversation at a family dinner or a group of friends turns to a very controversial political topic. Everyone is getting angry.

options.text: Pick a side and fight until everyone agrees with you.
options.motivation: You are destroying the "Social Arena." In the middle-class matrix, harmony is more important than being "right" in a casual setting.

options.text: "It’s clear there’s a lot of 'Quality' passion here. It’s a complex issue. Personally, I value [Neutral Point], but I’m more interested in how we can all enjoy this meal together."
options.motivation: This is Opening Doors. You act as the "Mediator." You acknowledge the passion but pivot back to the "Social Contract" of the gathering. People will be relieved and respect your maturity.

options.text: Sit in total silence and look annoyed with everyone.
options.motivation: This is "afstandelijk" (distanced). It makes you look like you think you are better than everyone else.

question_text: You are trying to buy a second-hand item (like a car or furniture) and the seller won't budge on a price that is too high.

options.text: "You’re crazy! This isn't worth half that much."
options.motivation: You’ve just insulted their "Merit." They will now refuse to sell to you out of spite, even if you offer more later.

options.text: "I truly respect the 'Quality' of what you have here. Based on my research, the 'Reliability' of the current market is [Price]. If you can meet me there, I can finish the deal today."
options.motivation: This is Commanding Respect. You compliment the item (Likeability) but stay firm on the data (Merit). You offer them a "Way Out" that lets them keep their dignity while giving you the price you want.

options.text: Just pay the full price because you hate conflict.
options.motivation: This is "submissive." You are losing your "Material Modesty" by overspending because you lack "Social Intelligence."

question_text: A project you were responsible for has failed, and it’s being discussed in a group meeting. Your mistake was the primary cause.

options.text: "I take full responsibility for the 'Quality' of this outcome. I've already identified the 'Reliability' gaps and have a plan to fix them."
options.motivation: This is Commanding Respect. A young man who admits a mistake without being "onderdanig" (submissive) or making excuses is seen as having immense "Internal Strength." You turn a failure into a demonstration of leadership.

options.text: "The software was glitchy and I didn't get the support I needed from the seniors."
options.motivation: This is "Low Status." Shifting blame makes you look weak and "unreliable." You lose the "Professional Mirror" of a man who can handle responsibility.

options.text: Stay silent and hope no one looks at you while the boss talks.
options.motivation: This is "submissive." Silence in a crisis looks like guilt without the courage to own it. It kills your "Likeability Factor."

question_text: Someone you had a disagreement with at the sports club posts a mocking, half-true story about you in a public group.

options.text: Ignore the post entirely, but continue to show up at the club with a "Solid Stance" and your usual "Quality" behavior.
options.motivation: This is the Superpower of Silence. By not reacting, you show that the lie has no "Merit." When your real-life behavior is consistently "Dignified," the public will eventually turn against the gossip-monger, not you.

options.text: Write a long, angry reply defending yourself and exposing their secrets too.
options.motivation: This is a "Social Navigation" disaster. You’ve just invited everyone into a "dirty" Arena. You lose your dignity and look just as unpolished as the attacker.

options.text: Call them privately and beg them to take it down.
options.motivation: This is "submissive." It shows you are easily shaken by what others think, which lowers your "Commanding Respect" power.

question_text: You see a peer (a friend) doing something unethical that could damage the reputation of your shared group or workplace.

options.text: "As a friend, I have to tell you: this is a breach of our 'Reliability.' I can’t support this, and for the 'Quality' of our group, you need to set this right before it goes further."
options.motivation: This is Dignified Citizenship. You are being a "True Peer." You protect the "Arena" and the friend at the same time by holding them to a higher standard of "Merit."

options.text: Report them secretly to the boss without talking to them first.
options.motivation: This is seen as "underhanded." In the middle-class matrix, a man is expected to have the courage to address a peer directly first.

options.text: Help them hide what they did because "loyalty comes first."
options.motivation: This is a "Low Status" move. You are now just as "unreliable" as they are. If you are caught, your "Professional Brand" is dead forever.

question_text: The company is facing layoffs, but you just received a high-end luxury item (like a car or watch) that you ordered months ago.

options.text: Keep it in the garage or "Private Arena" for now. Showing it off during a crisis lacks "Social Intelligence" and likeability.
options.motivation: This is Opening Doors. You show empathy for those struggling around you. It’s the "Secret of the Notable": you don't rub your success in the faces of those who are hurting.

options.text: Drive it to work every day and park it right in the front to show you are "Recession-proof."
options.motivation: This is "Elite Arrogance." It creates a massive wall between you and your peers. You will be hated, and your "Reliability" as a team player will vanish.

options.text: Sell it immediately at a loss because you feel guilty.
options.motivation: This is "submissive." You shouldn't be ashamed of your merit, but you should be "bescheiden" (modest) about the timing of its display.

question_text: A male peer has insulted your expertise in front of a client to make himself look better.

options.text: Wait until the client leaves, then say: "Your comment earlier wasn't about 'Quality'; it was about ego. I expect us to maintain a 'Professional Mirror' in the future. How do we ensure that?"
options.motivation: This is Commanding Respect. You address the behavior directly but professionally. You don't get "physical" or "loud," which shows you have high-level emotional control.

options.text: Punch them in the parking lot.
options.motivation: This is a total failure of "Social Navigation." You will be fired, and your reputation as an "unrefined" man will follow you everywhere.

options.text: Do nothing and hope the client didn't notice.
options.motivation: This is "submissive." If you don't set the boundary, the "Bully" will continue to use you as a stepping stone.

question_text: You are out at a bar and a friend you are with starts a fight. You are associated with him in the eyes of the onlookers.

options.text: Step in to de-escalate without using violence, then remove your friend from the situation immediately.
options.motivation: This is Dignified Citizenship. You are the "Adult." You protect the "Public Arena" and your own "Reliability" by being the one who ends the chaos.

options.text: Join the fight to show your friend you have his back.
options.motivation: This is "Low Status" behavior. You have just destroyed your "Professional Brand" for anyone who sees you.

options.text: Run away and pretend you don't know him.
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig" (submissive). While it saves your skin, it shows a lack of character and "Loyalty" within the "Social Contract."

question_text: You have been fired or asked to leave a role. You are meeting your team for the last time.

options.text: "I’ve valued the 'Quality' of our work together. I'm moving on to focus on new 'Merit' goals. I wish you all 'Reliability' and success."
options.motivation: This is the Lasting Impression. You leave with your head high. This makes you "Likeable" even in defeat, and ensures your network remains an open door for the future.

options.text: Tell everyone how much you hate the boss and how the company is sinking anyway.
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd." You look bitter and "unreliable." You are burning bridges that you might need to cross ten years from now.

options.text: Cry and ask everyone to sign a petition to keep you.
options.motivation: This is "submissive." It lacks the "Stance" of a man who knows his own value outside of a single job title.

question_text: A group of peers wants to take a "shortcut" that is legally grey but highly profitable. They want you to lead the move.

options.text: "The 'Quality' of my leadership is based on long-term 'Reliability.' This shortcut puts our entire 'Arena' at risk. We will find a 'Merit-based' way to get these results, or we won't do it at all."
options.motivation: This is Commanding Respect. You show that your "Merit" is not for sale. This makes you the man people trust with the biggest, most important projects. You become a "Pillar" of the community.

options.text: Do it, but make sure your name isn't on any of the paperwork.
options.motivation: This is "underhanded." It shows you are a coward. If it fails, your reputation is gone; if it succeeds, you are still a man who cannot trust himself.

options.text: Say yes because you are afraid the group will think you aren't "edgy" enough.
options.motivation: This is "submissive" to peer pressure. A dignified citizen follows his own compass, not the wind of the crowd.

question_text: You are at a networking event or a community meeting. You are introduced to a senior professional. How do you establish your "Likeability Factor" immediately?

options.text: Give a soft, polite nod and wait for them to lead the conversation.
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig" (submissive). It makes you "invisible." In the middle-class matrix, you want to be seen as a peer-in-the-making, not a bystander.

options.text: A firm handshake, direct eye contact, a warm smile, and: "It’s a pleasure to meet you. I’m [Name], I’ve been following your work on [Project]."
options.motivation: This is Graceful Authority. You show confidence (Commanding Respect) but add a personal touch of "Quality" by mentioning their work. You become the person they remember as "impressive and bright."

options.text: Give a high-pitched, giggly greeting to show how "friendly" and non-threatening you are.
options.motivation: This kills your Professional Brand. It signals a lack of maturity. People might find you "nice," but they won't open doors of "Merit" for you.

question_text: You are preparing for your first presentation. What is the "Middle Class" rule for a rising female professional's appearance?

options.text: Wear very heavy makeup and lots of "flashy" jewelry to show you have success.
options.motivation: This violates "Materiële Bescheidenheid" (Material Modesty). It’s distracting. You want people to focus on the Quality of your ideas, not the sparkle of your accessories.

options.text: A "Natural-Polished" look: neat hair, clean nails, and subtle accessories that don't make noise when you move.
options.motivation: This is the Professional Mirror. It signals "Reliability." It shows you are "verzorgd" (meticulous) and that you respect the "Arena" enough to be at your best.

options.text: Don’t brush your hair and wear an oversized hoodie to show you are "down-to-earth."
options.motivation: This looks "unverzorgd." It can be interpreted as a lack of discipline. Doors remain closed to those who look like they haven't "arrived" yet.

question_text: You are in a meeting or at a dinner table. How do you sit to project "Expertise"?

options.text: Sit on the edge of the chair, leaning in with your elbows on the table and chin in hands.
options.motivation: This looks "juvenile." It suggests you are a student listening to a lecture, rather than a "Dignified Citizen" contributing to a solution.

options.text: Sit upright, back against the chair, feet flat or crossed at the ankles, with your hands visible on the table or lap.
options.motivation: This is Commanding Respect. By occupying your chair fully and keeping your hands visible, you signal that you are "Solid." You look calm, reliable, and ready to lead.

options.text: Curl into a ball or sit on one leg to be "comfortable."
options.motivation: This is too "Private Arena." It signals that you don't understand the "Social Contract" of the professional space. You lose your authority.

question_text: You are asked for your opinion on a project. How do you respond?

options.text: "I’m sorry, I just think that maybe we could possibly try this other way? If that’s okay?"
options.motivation: This is "submissive" language. Using "sorry," "just," and "maybe" destroys your Likeability Factor because people don't trust the "Merit" of someone who sounds unsure of themselves.

options.text: "Based on the data, I advise we proceed with [Option B]. It ensures the 'Quality' we are aiming for."
options.motivation: This is Opening Doors. By using "I advise" and focusing on "Quality," you sound like an expert. People listen to women who speak with "Reliable" logic.

options.text: "I don't care, whatever you guys decide is fine with me."
options.motivation: This is "Passive." It signals that you don't have "Merit" to contribute. You become a follower, not a "Dignified Citizen."

question_text: You enter the office or a local shop. There are several people you don't know well.

options.text: Walk in silently and go straight to your destination to be "efficient."
options.motivation: You look "arrogant" or cold. You are missing the easiest way to build Social Intelligence.

options.text: Make brief eye contact with the room, give a warm smile, and a clear "Good morning, everyone."
options.motivation: This is the Belgian Superpower. It marks you as "one of us." It opens the door to future favors. The person who greets well is the person who gets helped first.

options.text: Shout "Hey guys!" and start a long story about your morning before anyone says hello.
options.motivation: This is "forced" and intrusive. It breaks the "Public Arena" peace. You’ll be seen as "too much" rather than "dignified."

question_text: You are at the coffee machine and a senior manager walks up.

options.text: Compliment their outfit and ask where they bought it.
options.motivation: Too personal, too fast. It can feel like you are focusing on "Material" things rather than "Merit."

options.text: "Good morning. I’m [Name] from [Department]. I really enjoyed the 'Quality' of your presentation yesterday—especially the point about [Detail]."
options.motivation: This is the Likeability Factor. You provide your name (Professional Brand) and a "Merit-based" compliment. You look like a rising peer who pays attention to "Quality."

options.text: Run away because you are afraid of saying the wrong thing.
options.motivation: You are closing a door. The "Dignified Citizen" knows that every interaction is a "Social Investment."

question_text: You are at lunch with a group of friends or colleagues. Your phone buzzes with a text.

options.text: Put the phone on the table and check every notification as it arrives.
options.motivation: This signals that the people in the "Public Arena" are less important than your "Private Arena." It kills your Likeability Factor.

options.text: Keep the phone in your bag or pocket. If you must check it, excuse yourself briefly: "I'm expecting an important 'Reliability' update, one second."
options.motivation: This shows Commanding Respect. It signals that you are "Fully Present." People value the "Quality" of your attention.

options.text: Answer the phone at the table and have a loud personal conversation.
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd." It’s a breach of the "Social Contract." You lose your "Dignity" in the eyes of the group.

question_text: A peer says, "You did a great job on that report!"

options.text: "Oh, it was nothing, I just got lucky."
options.motivation: This is "False Modesty." It dismisses your own "Merit." If you don't value your "Quality," why should they?

options.text: "Thank you! I’m really pleased with the 'Quality' we achieved. I put a lot of focus into the [Specific Detail]."
options.motivation: This is Dignified Citizenship. You accept the praise with grace, acknowledge your hard work (Merit), and reinforce your "Professional Brand."

options.text: "I know, I’m basically carrying this whole team."
options.motivation: This is "opscheppen" (boasting). It kills the Likeability Factor and makes you look like a poor teammate.

question_text: You are invited to a higher-level meeting or a neighborhood association dinner.

options.text: Enter through the side door and try to find a seat in the back so no one sees you.
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig" (submissive). It suggests you don't belong in the "Arena."

options.text: Walk through the main entrance, scan the room with a smile, greet the host, and choose a seat that allows you to be part of the conversation.
options.motivation: This is Commanding Respect. You show you are a "Dignified Citizen" who is comfortable in any "Public Arena." You look like someone who is "Rising."

options.text: Run in while looking at your watch and complaining about traffic.
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd." It shows a lack of "Reliability." You start your interaction in a "Chaos Mirror."

question_text: You are leaving a group setting.

options.text: Just wave from the door and shout "Bye!"
options.motivation: Too informal. It leaves the "Professional Mirror" unfinished.

options.text: Make eye contact with the key people, nod, and say: "It was a 'Quality' discussion. I look forward to our next steps. Have a pleasant evening."
options.motivation: This is the Lasting Impression. You end on a note of "Dignity." People will remember you as "Polished" and "Reliable."

options.text: Slip out without saying anything to anyone.
options.motivation: You leave a "Void." In the middle-class matrix, "Social Navigation" requires a clear "Closing" to maintain the relationship.

question_text: You arrive at a local neighborhood reception or a "borrel" (drinks). You see a group of three people you’d like to meet, but they are deep in conversation.

options.text: Stand very close to them and wait for someone to stop talking so you can jump in.
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig" (submissive). It makes you look like you are waiting for permission to exist. You become a "shadow" rather than a "Dignified Citizen."

options.text: Catch the eye of one person, give a warm smile, and say: "I couldn't help but overhear your interesting point about [Topic]. Mind if I join you?"
options.motivation: This is the Likeability Factor. You validate their conversation first (Quality), which makes them feel important. By asking "Mind if I join?", you show Graceful Authority. People love to include someone who shows genuine interest.

options.text: Start talking to your phone loudly nearby so they see how "busy" and important you are.
options.motivation: This is "forced." It creates a barrier. You look "afstandelijk" (distanced) and unapproachable. You’ve just closed the door to that group.

question_text: You are out for lunch with two colleagues or friends. The waiter arrives and asks, "Are we ready to order?" Everyone is still looking at their menus.

options.text: Say nothing and wait for the person with the "highest status" to speak first.
options.motivation: You remain a follower. In the middle-class matrix, taking a small initiative shows you have the "Reliability" to manage a situation.

options.text: "I think we need just one more minute, thank you. Shall we start with some sparkling water for the table while we decide?"
options.motivation: This is Commanding Respect. You’ve made a decision that helps the group and the waiter. You look like the "Host" of the moment—reliable, decisive, and gracious. People subconsciously trust the woman who manages the logistics.

options.text: Tell the waiter to go away because you’re busy talking.
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd" (unpolished). It shows a lack of respect for the "Arena" staff. A "Dignified Citizen" is always kind to those who serve.

question_text: You are at a fun professional-social event and take a great group photo. You want to post it on Instagram or LinkedIn.

options.text: Post it immediately and tag everyone, even if they have their eyes closed or look "unpolished."
options.motivation: You are damaging their "Professional Mirror." This kills your Likeability Factor. They will see you as someone who only cares about your own image, not the group's "Quality."

options.text: Send the photo to the group first: "Great shot! Does everyone feel 'Quality' about this? I’d love to post it and tag you."
options.motivation: This is Social Intelligence. You are protecting their "Mirror." By asking, you show you are a "Reliable" and respectful friend. People will trust you more because you care about their dignity.

options.text: Don't post anything; "Private Arena" stuff should never be shared.
options.motivation: In the 19-30 age group, being "invisible" can slow down your networking. It’s better to share "Quality" moments that show you are active in the community.

question_text: A group is talking about a local celebrity who just bought a massive mansion. The conversation turns to how much "everyone in this area" must earn.

options.text: Join in and start guessing everyone's salary at the table.
options.motivation: You are breaking the "Middle Class" taboo. It makes the "Arena" feel "dirty" and competitive. You look unrefined and "low status."

options.text: "It’s a beautiful property. I always appreciate when people invest in 'Quality' architecture. It adds a lot of 'Dignity' to the neighborhood, don't you think?"
options.motivation: This is Opening Doors. You pivot the talk from "Raw Money" (Taboo) to "Quality" and "Neighborhood Pride" (Safe/Inspiring). You look like a woman of substance who values the community.

options.text: Get up and leave the table because you hate talking about money.
options.motivation: This is "afstandelijk" (distanced). You can stay in the conversation without breaking the rules.

question_text: A man you don't know well at an event starts calling you "honey" or "sweetie" during a conversation.

options.text: Smile and ignore it because you don't want to be "difficult."
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig" (submissive). You are letting someone else define your "Professional Mirror." It lowers your authority in the room.

options.text: Maintain a warm but "Solid Stance," and use their name firmly: "Actually, [Name], I’d prefer to keep our conversation on the 'Quality' of the project. Now, regarding [Topic]..."
options.motivation: This is Graceful Authority. You set a boundary without "shouting." By using their name, you remind them of your "Dignity." You look strong, likable, and impossible to "push around."

options.text: Scream at him in front of everyone to teach him a lesson.
options.motivation: This ruins the "Social Arena." While he was wrong, your "loss of control" makes you look unpolished to others who didn't hear the original comment.

question_text: You are at a local event and you’ve finished your conversation with a group. You need to move on to see someone else.

options.text: "I have to go now, this conversation is getting a bit long. Bye!"
options.motivation: This kills the Likeability Factor. It sounds "arrogant." You’ve just closed the door you spent 20 minutes opening.

options.text: "It’s been a 'Quality' conversation, thank you. I promised to catch [Name] before the end of the night. I hope we can continue this soon!"
options.motivation: This is Dignified Citizenship. You validate them (Dignity), give a "Reliable" reason for leaving, and leave the door open for the future. You leave them feeling good about themselves, which makes them like you.

options.text: Just walk away when they turn to talk to someone else.
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd." You leave a "messy" ending. A "Dignified Citizen" always finishes the interaction with a clean "Full Stop."

question_text: You’ve saved up and bought a high-quality designer handbag or coat. A friend says, "Wow, that’s beautiful! It must have cost a fortune!"

options.text: "Actually, I got a great deal on it. It’s an investment in my 'Professional Mirror'—quality items open doors, you know?"
options.motivation: This is a bit too clinical. You sound like a career coach rather than a friend. While true, it lacks the Likeability Factor.

options.text: "Thank you! I really value the 'Quality' and the timeless design. I plan to keep it for many years, so for me, the durability was the main selling point."
options.motivation: This is the Secret of the Notable. You focus on "Craftsmanship" and "Sustainability." You aren't boasting about the price; you are showing that you are a woman of "Substance" who makes wise, long-term decisions.

options.text: "It was actually quite cheap, I found it at a local market for ten euros."
options.motivation: This is "False Modesty." If people recognize the brand, they will think you are dishonest. A Dignified Citizen is honest about her choices without being flashy.

question_text: You just returned from a very high-end vacation. Someone at a social gathering asks, "How was your trip? I heard that place is incredibly exclusive."

options.text: "It was the most expensive place I've ever been, the service was truly elite."
options.motivation: This is "opscheppen" (boasting). You are defining the trip by its "cost," which makes others feel excluded. You lose the Likeability Factor immediately.

options.text: "It was a 'Quality' experience. What I appreciated most was the incredible attention to detail and the peaceful 'Arena' they created. It was very restorative."
options.motivation: This is Graceful Authority. You speak about the feeling and the standard of the trip. You make it sound like a deserved reward for hard work rather than just a display of wealth.

options.text: "It was alright, but the Wi-Fi was terrible so it wasn't really worth the money."
options.motivation: This sounds ungrateful. A woman of "Dignity" acknowledges her success with a smile, rather than complaining about luxury.

question_text: You’ve had a major win at work and want to buy something to celebrate. What is the "Middle Class" standard for a young woman?

options.text: Something with the largest possible logo so everyone knows you’ve "made it."
options.motivation: Large logos are often seen as "brash" (nouveau riche). In the Belgian matrix, "Refinement" is found in the "Hidden Detail"—quality that only those with similar standards will recognize.

options.text: A piece of high-quality jewelry or a professional tool (like a premium pen or tech) that is elegant and functional.
options.motivation: This is Commanding Respect. It signals that you value "Merit" and "Permanence." You are building a "Professional Mirror" that reflects a reliable and serious character.

options.text: Buying a round of drinks for the whole bar and shouting, "The next promotion is on me!"
options.motivation: While fun, this is "unverzorgd" (unpolished). It lacks the "Bescheidenheid" (modesty) that keeps you likable in the long run.

question_text: A client or partner gives you an expensive gift that feels a bit "too much" for the level of your relationship.

options.text: Return it immediately and tell them it’s inappropriate.
options.motivation: This is "afstandelijk" (distanced). It can be seen as an insult to their "Merit." It closes a door that could have stayed open.

options.text: "This is a very 'Quality' gesture, thank you. I value the 'Reliability' of our partnership. Let’s share this with the whole team so everyone can enjoy it."
options.motivation: This is Social Intelligence. By sharing the gift (like a box of luxury chocolates or wine), you remove the personal pressure and turn it into a "Social Contract" win. You look generous and professional.

options.text: Keep it for yourself and don't tell anyone.
options.motivation: This can lead to "Social Navigation" problems later. If it looks like a "bribe," your "Merit" is at risk. Transparency is the middle-class shield.

question_text: You’ve invited your first group of professional peers to your new home. How do you show "Material Modesty"?

options.text: Hire a professional catering team and waitresses to show you are "Elite."
options.motivation: This is "Elite Arrogance." It makes your peers feel uncomfortable and "onderdanig." You lose the Likeability Factor of being a peer.

options.text: Prepare a high-quality, simple meal yourself. Focus on the 'Quality' of the ingredients and the 'Social Arena' of the conversation.
options.motivation: This is Dignified Citizenship. You show that you are a "Maker." By doing it yourself, you show respect for your guests' time and presence. It makes you incredibly likable and respected.

options.text: Order pizza and tell everyone to bring their own chairs.
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd." It suggests you don't value the "Social Contract" of hosting. It lacks the "Polished" mirror of a rising professional.

question_text: You are explaining a project you designed. A male peer interrupts to explain your own logic back to you, as if you didn't understand it.

options.text: Let him finish and then say: "Exactly, thank you for clarifying what I just said."
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig" (submissive). It gives away your Authority. You are letting him occupy your "Merit" space.

options.text: "I appreciate you reinforcing that point, [Name]. As I was saying, the 'Quality' of the data also shows that..."
options.motivation: This is Graceful Authority. You acknowledge him briefly (Social Intelligence) but immediately pivot back to your own voice. You don't get angry; you simply stay "Solid." You look like the expert who is in control of the room.

options.text: Stop the meeting and accuse him of being sexist.
options.motivation: This is a "Social Navigation" explosion. It makes the "Arena" feel unsafe and "unverzorgd." You lose your likeability and look "difficult" rather than "dignified."

question_text: You are in a performance review. You’ve delivered high-quality results, but your manager says there is "no budget" for a raise this year.

options.text: "I understand the 'Reliability' of the current budget. However, based on the 'Quality' I’ve delivered, I’d like to discuss a 'Merit-based' bonus or a review in six months."
options.motivation: This is Commanding Respect. You don't accept a "No" as a dead end. You use "Professional Language" to keep the door open. You look like a woman who knows the value of her own work (Substance).

options.text: Start crying and tell them you really need the money for your rent.
options.motivation: This is "needs-based" rather than "merit-based." It blurs the "Privé en Zakelijk" (Private/Business) boundary and makes you look "unreliable" for high-level roles.

options.text: Say "Okay, no problem" and never bring it up again.
options.motivation: You are failing to defend your own "Merit." A Dignified Citizen is a partner in her own success, not a passive observer.

question_text: A close friend in your social circle asks you to use your professional connections to get them a job for which they are clearly not qualified.

options.text: Do it anyway because you don't want to lose the friendship.
options.motivation: You are risking your own "Reliability." If the friend fails, your "Professional Brand" is damaged. In the middle class, Merit comes before "favoritism."

options.text: "I value our friendship too much to put it at risk. I’d love to help you polish your CV to match the 'Quality' they look for, but I can't put my name on an intro until we bridge that gap."
options.motivation: This is Opening Doors through honesty. You offer a "Merit-based" solution (polishing the CV) while protecting your own "Arena." You look like a woman of integrity and a loyal friend.

options.text: Just ignore their messages until they stop asking.
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd" (unpolished). It leaves a "messy" ending to the social contract. A dignified citizen is direct and clear.

question_text: Two women in your friend group are arguing loudly at a dinner party. The atmosphere is becoming "dirty" and uncomfortable.

options.text: Pick the side of the friend you like more and join the argument.
options.motivation: You are destroying the "Social Arena." You lose your "Dignity" and look like a "troublemaker."

options.text: "There’s clearly a lot of 'Quality' passion here, but let's keep the 'Peace of the Table.' Why don't we pivot back to [Neutral Topic] and discuss this privately later?"
options.motivation: This is Graceful Authority. You act as the "Anchor." You remind them of the "Social Contract" of the evening. People will feel relieved and look to you as the natural leader of the group.

options.text: Sit in silence and keep drinking your wine while looking at your phone.
options.motivation: You are being passive. A Dignified Citizen has a responsibility to the "Public Arena" they are in.

question_text: A person who thinks they are "Elite" is being condescending to a waiter or a younger person in your presence.

options.text: "I’ve always found that the 'Quality' of a person is shown by how they treat everyone in the 'Arena.' Let's keep our conversation respectful, shall we?"
options.motivation: This is Commanding Respect. You are setting a "Moral Standard." You aren't shouting; you are reminding them of the rules of Dignified Citizenship. You look incredibly strong and highly likable to everyone else in the room.

options.text: Laugh along with them so they think you are "Elite" too.
options.motivation: You are losing your "Merit." You look like an "imposter" trying to fit in by being mean. It kills your Likeability Factor.

options.text: Say nothing and feel bad for the waiter.
options.motivation: You are being "onderdanig" to the bully. A dignified woman uses her voice to protect the "Dignity" of the Arena.

question_text: You accidentally sent a message criticizing a colleague's "Quality" of work to the colleague themselves instead of your friend.

options.text: Delete the message immediately and pretend your phone was hacked.
options.motivation: This is "unreliable." In the middle-class matrix, everyone knows this excuse is a lie. It destroys your Likeability Factor because it shows you lack the courage to be honest.

options.text: Call or go to them immediately: "I am deeply sorry. That message was unpolished and 'unverzorgd.' It was a lapse in my 'Social Navigation,' and I’d like to discuss the actual 'Quality' issue with you directly and professionally."
options.motivation: This is Radical Accountability. You own the mistake (Dignity) but pivot the conversation to a professional "Merit" discussion. People respect a woman who doesn't hide when she makes a mess.

options.text: Avoid them for the rest of the week and hope they don't bring it up.
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig" (submissive). It allows a "poisonous" atmosphere to grow in the "Arena." You lose your Graceful Authority.

question_text: A "dirty" rumor is circulating about your personal life in your professional or community circle.

options.text: Post a long, emotional video on social media explaining your side and crying.
options.motivation: This blurs the "Privé en Zakelijk" boundary in a messy way. You are inviting the "Public Arena" into your bedroom. You lose your Commanding Respect power.

options.text: Stay "Solid." Don't address the rumor. Instead, increase the "Quality" and "Visibility" of your contributions. Let your "Merit" be so loud that the rumor becomes "unreliable" by comparison.
options.motivation: This is the Superpower of Substance. In the middle class, "Results" are the ultimate defense. A Dignified Citizen knows that gossip dies when it hits a wall of consistent, high-quality character.

options.text: Find the person who started it and have a loud confrontation in the lobby.
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd." It gives the rumor more "Airtime." You look like you are part of the drama rather than above it.

question_text: Your team made a mistake that cost a client money. Your manager is looking for a "head to roll." You are the most senior woman on the junior team.
