options.text: "I take full responsibility as the lead for the 'Quality' of this output. I have identified the 'Reliability' gaps and I will ensure the team fixes this by tomorrow."
options.motivation: This is Graceful Authority. By "taking the hit" for the team, you prove you are a leader. The juniors will be intensely loyal to you (Likeability), and the manager will see you as "Executive Material" (Merit).

options.text: Point out exactly which junior made the specific typo that caused the error.
options.motivation: This is "Low Status." It shows you are a "Manager" but not a "Leader." You lose the trust of your peers and look "unreliable" under pressure.

options.text: Resign immediately so you don't have to deal with the fallout.
options.motivation: This is "submissive." It suggests you don't have the "Internal Quality" to handle the weight of the name.

question_text: You just won a major award or promotion, but a close friend in the same circle just lost their job or suffered a loss.

options.text: Downplay your success: "Oh, it was just luck, it’s not even a big deal."
options.motivation: This is "False Modesty." It can feel insulting to the friend because you are dismissing something they would love to have.

options.text: "I’m so grateful for this 'Merit' win, but my focus today is on our friendship. I’m here for you—how can I best support your 'Reliability' right now?"
options.motivation: This is Social Intelligence. You acknowledge your "Quality" but pivot the "Social Arena" to their needs. You look like a woman of "Substance" who isn't blinded by her own light.

options.text: Post 20 photos of your trophy and celebrate loudly while they are sitting next to you.
options.motivation: This is "opscheppen" (boasting). It kills the Likeability Factor. It shows a total lack of empathy and "Social Navigation."

question_text: You discover that the "Quality" of a product your company is selling is dangerous, but the "Elite" at the top want to keep it quiet to protect profits.

options.text: "My 'Reliability' is tied to the 'Quality' of what we provide. I cannot put my 'Name' on this. We must follow the 'Merit-based' safety protocols or I cannot remain in this 'Arena'."
options.motivation: This is Commanding Respect. You are setting a "Moral Anchor." Even if it costs you the job, your reputation as a woman of Integrity will open much bigger doors in the future. You become a "Notable" of character.

options.text: Do it anyway because you need the paycheck.
options.motivation: Once you compromise your "Merit" for money, you are no longer a "Dignified Citizen." You become "unreliable" to yourself and the world.

options.text: Leak it anonymously to the press and stay in the job.
options.motivation: While it saves the public, it is "underhanded." In the middle-class matrix, a woman of substance is expected to have the courage to state her position clearly first.

question_text: You are at a business lunch where there is one young woman and two young men. Who introduces themselves first to establish a "Quality" atmosphere?

options.text: The men should wait for the woman to speak first out of "Chivalry."
options.motivation: This is a bit archaic for a merit-based "Arena." It can make the woman feel "Elite-distanced" or like a guest rather than a professional peer.

options.text: Whoever is the "Host" or the first to make eye contact should initiate: "Good afternoon, I’m [Name]. It’s a pleasure to have us all here to discuss [Project]."
options.motivation: This is Graceful Authority. It focuses on the "Function" and the "Merit" of the meeting. It establishes everyone as equal "Dignified Citizens" working toward a shared "Quality" goal.

options.text: No one introduces themselves; just start looking at the menu in silence.
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd" (unpolished). It creates an awkward "Chaos Mirror." You miss the chance to open the door to a solid professional relationship.

question_text: A male and female colleague are going to a client meeting together. How do they ensure their "Likeability Factor" is synchronized?

options.text: They should both wear identical suits so they look like a "Unit."
options.motivation: This can look "forced" or like a uniform. The middle class values "Individual Merit" within a shared standard of "Neatness."

options.text: Both follow the "Plus-One" rule: One level of neatness above the client. Clean, well-fitted, "Verzorgd" clothing that signals "Reliability" without being flashy.
options.motivation: This is the Professional Mirror. When both colleagues look equally "Polished," it signals that the company’s "Standard" is consistent. It makes the client trust the "Quality" of the whole team.

options.text: The man wears a suit and the woman wears a tracksuit to show "Diversity."
options.motivation: This breaks the "Social Contract" of the meeting. One person looks "Elite" and the other looks "Unverzorgd." It creates a "Split Mirror" that confuses the client.

question_text: A young man is speaking to a young female colleague he has just met.

options.text: Call her "Miss [Last Name]" until she gives him permission to use her first name.
options.motivation: In the 19-30 "Arena," this can feel "stiff" or "afstandelijk." It creates a barrier to the "Likeability Factor."

options.text: Start with a respectful "Hello, [Name]," and mirror her tone. If she is professional but warm, you do the same.
options.motivation: This is Adaptive Linguistics. By using her name but keeping the tone professional, you establish "Graceful Authority." You show you respect her "Merit" as a peer.

options.text: Call her "Hey girl" or "Sweetheart" to make the environment "relaxed."
options.motivation: This is "Low Status" behavior. It is a major breach of "Dignity." You will be seen as unrefined and "unreliable" for serious professional work.

question_text: A mixed group is working in a shared space. How do they maintain "Social Intelligence"?

options.text: Everyone should spread their bags and coats over extra chairs to "claim their territory."
options.motivation: This is "Low Status" and selfish. It disrupts the "Public Arena" and makes you unlikable to everyone around you.

options.text: Keep your personal items (Private Arena) contained to your desk area. Keep walkways clear and maintain a "Solid" but respectful posture.
options.motivation: This is Dignified Citizenship. By respecting the "Shared Space," you signal that you are a "Reliable" and organized professional. People want to work near the person who maintains "Order."

options.text: Sit on the floor to be "different" and "creative."
options.motivation: As a young professional, sitting on the floor can make you look "onderdanig" (submissive) compared to those in chairs. It’s better to stay at "Eye Level" with your peers.

question_text: You walk into the office kitchen where a colleague of the opposite gender is making coffee.

options.text: Look at the floor and wait for them to finish so you don't "interrupt."
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig." It makes the atmosphere awkward and "stiff."

options.text: Make brief eye contact, give a warm smile, and say: "Good morning! How is the [Project/Day] going so far?"
options.motivation: This is the Belgian Superpower. It breaks the ice and opens the door to "Social Navigation." It shows you are a confident, likable peer who is comfortable in the "Shared Arena."

options.text: Walk in and start complaining about how tired you are and how much you hate Mondays.
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd." You are bringing "Negative Quality" into the room. No one wants to open doors for the person who brings the mood down.

question_text: A male colleague sends a female colleague a work-related question on Sunday evening.

options.text: She must answer immediately to show she is "Hardworking."
options.motivation: This blurs the Privé en Zakelijk boundary. It sets a precedent that your "Private Arena" is always open, which can lead to burnout.

options.text: He should wait until Monday, or she should wait until Monday to reply: "Good morning! Regarding your question from yesterday..."
options.motivation: This is Commanding Respect. It shows that both respect the "Social Contract" of the weekend. It reinforces the "Reliability" of professional hours.

options.text: He sends the message and then calls her three times when she doesn't answer.
options.motivation: This is intrusive and "unverzorgd." It shows a total lack of "Social Intelligence." You are closing doors by being "pushy."

question_text: A mixed group of four people is getting into a small elevator.

options.text: The men should push to the back to let the women stand near the buttons.
options.motivation: Too much "fuss." In a small "Public Arena," the most "Reliable" thing is to move efficiently so the door can close.

options.text: Whoever is closest to the buttons should hold the "Open" button for the others, and everyone should stand with a "Solid Stance," facing the door.
options.motivation: This is Social Intelligence. It’s about "Function." By being helpful and respecting personal space, you look like a "Dignified" group of peers.

options.text: Everyone should keep talking loudly about private office gossip.
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd." You never know who else is in the "Arena." You are risking your "Professional Brand" for a small talk.

question_text: A male peer wants to compliment a female peer on a "Merit" win.

options.text: "You look great today, that dress really suits you."
options.motivation: In the "Professional Arena," this is a distraction. It shifts the focus from "Merit" to "Material Appearance." It can be seen as "unverzorgd."

options.text: "That was a high-'Quality' presentation, [Name]. The way you handled the data was very 'Reliable'."
options.motivation: This is the Likeability Factor. You are praising her "Merit" and "Expertise." This builds a bond of mutual respect between peers.

options.text: Say nothing because you are afraid it will be "misunderstood."
options.motivation: Silence doesn't build any "Social Capital." The "Dignified Citizen" knows how to give a "Clean Compliment" based on work.

question_text: A young man and woman are meeting a client at a restaurant. Who enters first?

options.text: The man must hold the door and wait for the woman to enter, then find the table.
options.motivation: This is the "Social Standard," but in a "Mixed Merit" team, it can look like the man is the "Assistant" or the "Bodyguard."

options.text: Whoever is leading the client relationship should enter first to greet the host and lead the way to the table.
options.motivation: This is Graceful Authority. It’s based on "Role" and "Merit." It shows the client that your team is organized by "Competence" rather than just tradition.

options.text: They should race each other to the door to see who is faster.
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd." It’s juvenile and ruins the "Professional Mirror" for the client.

question_text: The meeting is over. How does the mixed group finish the interaction?

options.text: The men stand up and the women stay seated.
options.motivation: This creates a "Split Level" in the room. It’s better if everyone moves as "Peers."

options.text: Everyone stands, makes eye contact, and offers a brief: "Thank you for the 'Quality' time. I look forward to our 'Reliability' on the next steps."
options.motivation: This is the Lasting Impression. It seals the "Social Contract" for the whole group. You all leave as "Dignified Citizens" and "Reliable" partners.

options.text: Everyone just walks out while looking at their phones.
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd." You are leaving a "messy" ending. A "Dignified" exit ensures the door stays open for the next collaboration.

question_text: You are meeting a female colleague or potential business partner for a drink. You arrive at the terrace first.

options.text: Sit down, order your drink, and start scrolling on your phone until she arrives.
options.motivation: This is "afstandelijk" (distanced). You are missing the chance to be the "Host." It makes the start of the meeting feel functional rather than "Quality."

options.text: Stay standing or rise when you see her approach, offer the better seat (view/comfort), and say: "I’m glad you could make it. Shall we see what they have on the menu?"
options.motivation: This is Commanding Respect. By facilitating her arrival, you show you are "Solid" and aware of the "Arena." It’s not about being a servant; it's about being a "Dignified Leader" of the situation.

options.text: Wave her over from your seat and shout, "Hey! Over here!"
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd" (unpolished). It’s too loud for a public space and lacks the "Refinement" that makes a woman feel respected as a peer.

question_text: You are walking to a meeting or a restaurant with a female peer. The sidewalk is narrow.

options.text: Walk slightly ahead of her to show you know the way.
options.motivation: This can feel "Elite-distanced." It suggests you are more interested in the destination than the person you are with.

options.text: Match her pace, stay on the "street side" of the sidewalk, and maintain the conversation at a comfortable volume.
options.motivation: This is Social Intelligence. Matching pace shows "Peer Equality." Staying on the street side is a "Hidden Detail" of traditional respect that signals you are a "Reliable" and thoughtful man.

options.text: Walk behind her so you can check your emails while you walk.
options.motivation: This kills the Likeability Factor. It is dismissive and makes the "Social Navigation" feel "unverzorgd."

question_text: You have had a "Quality" networking lunch with a female peer. The bill arrives.

options.text: Insist on paying for her and say, "I never let a lady pay."
options.motivation: In a 19-30 merit-based "Arena," this can sometimes feel "onderdanig" (condescending) to her professional status. It can blur the "Professional Mirror."

options.text: "I’d like to take this one. It was a very 'Quality' discussion." If she insists on splitting, say: "Then let's split it. I value our 'Reliability' as partners."
options.motivation: This is Adaptive Linguistics. You show the "Generosity of the Notable" by offering first, but you respect her "Dignity" and independence by agreeing to split if she asks. You look like a balanced peer.

options.text: Sit back and wait for her to suggest splitting the bill.
options.motivation: This lacks "Authority." The "Dignified Citizen" takes the initiative to resolve the logistics of the "Arena."

question_text: You are in the office kitchen and a female colleague is reaching for something in a high cabinet or a crowded fridge.

options.text: Step in immediately, move her aside, and say, "Let me do that for you."
options.motivation: This can be seen as intrusive. You are invading her "Private Arena" without being asked.

options.text: Give her space first. If she seems to struggle, ask: "May I help you with that?" and step in only if she says yes.
options.motivation: This is Graceful Authority. You offer help (Likeability) but respect her "Personal Arena" (Dignity). You look like a man who is helpful but not "pushy."

options.text: Stand right behind her and wait for her to move.
options.motivation: This is a "Social Navigation" failure. It creates discomfort and feels "unverzorgd."

question_text: You are with a female peer in a social group. Someone asks what you think of her recent work.

options.text: "She’s the smartest woman I know, and she looks great in that suit, too."
options.motivation: Mixing "Merit" with "Appearance" in public is a major taboo. It devalues her work. You lose your Commanding Respect power.

options.text: "Her focus on the 'Quality' of [Project] has been impressive. The 'Reliability' she brings to the team is exactly what we needed."
options.motivation: This is the Likeability Factor. You are building her "Professional Brand." By praising her "Substance" to others, you look like a strong, secure man who isn't afraid of a peer's success.

options.text: "I don't really pay attention to what she does."
options.motivation: This makes you look "afstandelijk" (distanced) and unsupportive. It closes doors to future collaborations.

question_text: A social event ends late in the evening. You and a female peer are leaving at the same time.

options.text: Say "Bye!" and run to your car immediately.
options.motivation: While "efficient," it ignores the "Social Intelligence" of the situation. It can feel "unverzorgd."

options.text: "It was a 'Quality' night. I’m heading to my car/the station—may I walk you to yours to ensure everything is 'Reliable'?"
options.motivation: This is Dignified Citizenship. You offer a "Secret of the Gentleman" (safety) without being "forced." You look like a man who cares about the "Shared Arena" until the very end.

options.text: Insist on driving her home even if she says she has her own bike/car.
options.motivation: This is "pushy." It ignores her "Dignity" and independence. A "Notable" man offers once and respects the answer.

question_text: You’ve bought a high-end smartphone or laptop. A female colleague says, "That’s a fancy piece of tech! Was it expensive?"

options.text: "It’s the top-of-the-line model. I believe in having the best tools money can buy."
options.motivation: This sounds a bit like "Elite Arrogance." While focused on tools, it emphasizes the "power" of buying rather than the "Quality" of the work.

options.text: "I’ve found it really improves my 'Productivity.' The 'Reliability' of the hardware makes my workflow much smoother, which I value."
options.motivation: This is the Secret of the Notable. You justify the expense through "Utility" and "Merit." You show her that you are a man who invests in his "Expertise" rather than just showing off. It’s highly likable and commands respect.

options.text: "Actually, I think it’s a bit of a waste of money, I just bought it because everyone else did."
options.motivation: This is "False Modesty." It makes you look like you don't have a "Solid Stance" or your own opinion. A dignified citizen owns his choices.

question_text: You and a female peer are discussing holiday plans. You just returned from a high-end ski trip or a boutique hotel stay.

options.text: "The resort was incredibly exclusive. The price per night was crazy, but you get what you pay for."
options.motivation: This is "opscheppen" (boasting). Focusing on exclusivity and price creates a barrier. It kills the Likeability Factor because it sounds like you are trying to impress her with cash rather than character.

options.text: "It was a very 'Quality' experience. The attention to detail in the service was impressive, and it was a great 'Arena' to truly disconnect."
options.motivation: This is Graceful Authority. You use "Quality" as the codeword for success. You focus on the experience and the standard, which makes you a "Dignified Citizen" with refined taste.

options.text: "It was okay, but I've seen better. I'm used to higher standards."
options.motivation: This is "Elite Distance." It makes you look difficult to please and arrogant.

question_text: You are inviting a female peer to a meeting outside the office. You want to choose a place that shows you have "arrived."

options.text: Pick the most expensive, "flashy" rooftop bar in the city.
options.motivation: This can feel "forced" or like a date, which blurs the Privé en Zakelijk (Private/Business) boundary. It might make her feel uncomfortable in a professional "Arena."

options.text: Pick a quiet, high-quality bistro or a "Notable" café known for its history and excellent coffee.
options.motivation: This is Social Intelligence. You prioritize "Quality" of conversation and "Dignity" of the space. It shows you have "Refined Taste" without needing to shout about it. You become a man who knows the "Secret Spots" of the city.

options.text: Pick a noisy fast-food joint to show how "down-to-earth" you are.
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd." It suggests you don't value her time or the "Merit" of the meeting.

question_text: A female peer shows up in a new, clearly expensive car or with a high-end designer item.

options.text: "Wow, you must be making the big bucks now! How much did that set you back?"
options.motivation: You are breaking the "Middle Class" taboo of talking about raw money. It makes the "Arena" feel competitive and "dirty." It kills your Likeability Factor.

options.text: "That’s a very 'Quality' choice. I’ve always admired the 'Reliability' of that brand. It suits your 'Professional Mirror' perfectly."
options.motivation: This is Commanding Respect. You acknowledge her success by praising her "Taste" and "Merit." You look like a secure man who is happy to see a peer succeed.

options.text: Pretend you don't notice it at all so she doesn't think you're "looking."
options.motivation: Silence can feel "afstandelijk" (distanced). A brief, dignified compliment on the "Quality" of the item is the mark of a "Dignified Citizen."

question_text: You’ve had a financial win and you are out with a female peer. You want to show "Material Modesty" while being generous.

options.text: "I’m paying for everything tonight because I just got a massive bonus."
options.motivation: This can make her feel "onderdanig" (submissive). It links your generosity to your bank balance rather than your friendship.

options.text: "It’s been a very 'Quality' month, and I’d like to treat us to this lunch to celebrate our 'Reliability' as a team."
options.motivation: This is Dignified Citizenship. You share the success by framing it as a "Team Celebration." You look like a man of "Abundance" who uses his success to strengthen the "Social Arena."

options.text: Don't offer to pay at all, and make sure she pays her exact share down to the cent.
options.motivation: This is "petty." A man who fights over cents loses his "Dignity" in the eyes of his peers.

question_text: A female colleague or partner is presenting a plan to a group, and she makes a significant data error that you know will lead to a "Quality" failure later.

options.text: Interrupt her immediately to fix the error before she says anything else.
options.motivation: This is "brash" and ruins the "Social Navigation" of her presentation. It makes you look like you are trying to "dominate" the Arena rather than help. You lose the Likeability Factor.

options.text: Wait for a natural break or the end, then say: "That was a very 'Quality' overview. I noticed one 'Reliability' detail regarding [Data Point]—should we double-check that to ensure the 'Masterpiece' is perfect?"
options.motivation: This is Graceful Authority. You praise the work first (Social Intelligence), then offer the correction as a way to protect the "Quality" of the group's result. You look like a supportive, meticulous peer.

options.text: Say nothing and hope someone else notices it so you don't look like the "bad guy."
options.motivation: This is "unreliable." If the error stands, the "Arena" suffers. A Dignified Citizen has the courage to speak up to protect the standard.

question_text: A female peer is very upset or angry about a professional setback and is speaking to you with a lot of emotion in a public space.

options.text: Tell her she’s being "emotional" and needs to calm down.
options.motivation: This is a "Social Navigation" disaster. It is dismissive and "Elite-distanced." You will be seen as cold and unrefined, and you will lose her respect forever.

options.text: Maintain a "Solid Stance," listen without interrupting, and then say: "I hear the frustration. Let's look at the 'Merit' of the next steps together once we’ve had a moment to breathe."
options.motivation: This is the Velvet Anchor. You acknowledge the feeling (Likeability) but firmly point back to the "Merit" and the "Work" (Commanding Respect). By staying calm, you act as the "Dignified Anchor" for the situation.

options.text: Start crying with her to show how "empathetic" you are.
options.motivation: While empathetic, if you are in a professional "Arena," you lose your "Authority." A man of substance provides "Reliability" through stability.

question_text: A female peer or manager is consistently texting you about work "Private Arena" (evenings/weekends).

options.text: Ignore her completely until Monday morning without saying anything.
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd." It leaves the "Social Contract" hanging. It can be seen as "afstandelijk" (distanced).

options.text: Reply once politely: "I want to give this the 'Quality' attention it deserves. I’ll dive into this first thing Monday during our 'Reliability' hours. Have a great weekend!"
options.motivation: This is Commanding Respect. You set a clear boundary (Dignity) but frame it as a commitment to "Quality." You look like a man who values his work and his time equally.

options.text: Reply immediately and spend your whole Sunday working for her.
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig" (submissive). You are devaluing your own "Private Arena." If you don't respect your boundaries, no one else will.

question_text: You and a female peer have two completely different ideas on how to finish a project. She is very attached to her version.

options.text: Give in and do it her way because "the woman is always right."
options.motivation: This is "submissive" and patronizing. It suggests you don't value the "Merit" of the project. It’s not how "Dignified Citizens" interact.

options.text: "We both want the highest 'Quality' outcome. Let's test the 'Reliability' of both ideas against our goals. I value your 'Expertise'—let's see which one serves the 'Masterpiece' best."
options.motivation: This is Opening Doors. You move the conflict away from "Person vs. Person" and toward "Idea vs. Goal." You show you respect her "Expertise," which keeps the Likeability Factor high while you fight for the best result.

options.text: Tell her that her idea is illogical and yours is objectively better.
options.motivation: Too blunt. It creates friction in the "Social Arena." A "Notable" man wins through "Logic wrapped in Grace."

question_text: You and a female peer are no longer working together because of a disagreement or a change in company "Arena."

options.text: "I’ve appreciated the 'Quality' of our shared work. Even though our 'Reliability' paths are diverging, I wish you total 'Merit' in your next steps."
options.motivation: This is the Lasting Impression. You leave as a "Dignified Citizen." By wishing her well despite the conflict, you show immense "Internal Quality." The door stays open for a future "Social Navigation."

options.text: Tell everyone in the office that she was the reason for the failure.
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd." It makes you look bitter and "low status." Your "Professional Brand" takes a bigger hit than hers.

options.text: Just vanish and block her on LinkedIn.
options.motivation: This is "unpolished." It leaves a "Void" in your reputation. A man of substance finishes what he starts with a clean "Full Stop."

question_text: In a heated community or office group chat, a female peer accuses you of "ignoring her input" on a major project, implying you are biased. The whole group is watching.

options.text: Fire back a list of every time she was late or her work lacked "Quality."
options.motivation: This is a "Social Navigation" disaster. You look defensive and petty. By attacking her "Merit" in response to a personal attack, you make the "Arena" feel "dirty." You lose the Likeability Factor.

options.text: "I value the 'Quality' of every voice in this 'Arena.' If there was a gap in our 'Reliability,' I want to fix it. Let's meet tomorrow to ensure your 'Merit' is fully reflected in the final 'Masterpiece'."
options.motivation: This is Commanding Respect. You stay "Solid" (Dignity) and move the conflict to a private, professional space. You show you are a man who cares about "Quality" more than your ego. You look like a leader.

options.text: Post a public apology saying you’ll do whatever she wants from now on.
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig" (submissive). It suggests you lack the "Internal Quality" to defend a fair process. You lose your "Authority" as a peer.

question_text: A female friend in your professional circle is involved in a public scandal (e.g., a "Private Arena" mistake that went viral). People are distancing themselves from her.

options.text: "I’ve seen the 'Quality' of your work for years. I don't judge a 'Merit-based' partner by a single 'Social Navigation' error. How can I help you maintain your 'Professional Mirror' right now?"
options.motivation: This is Dignified Citizenship. You show "Loyalty" to the "Merit" of the person. By standing by her when others run, you demonstrate immense "Internal Strength." This builds a bond of trust that lasts a lifetime.

options.text: Block her immediately so your own "Professional Brand" doesn't get "dirty."
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd" (unpolished). It shows you are a "Fair-weather Friend." The middle-class matrix respects "Reliability" in bad times as much as good times.

options.text: Post a public statement condemning her actions so everyone knows you are "Moral."
options.motivation: This is "opscheppen" (boasting) about your own morality. It’s seen as "Elite Arrogance." A man of substance doesn't need to step on others to look tall.

question_text: A female peer who has "Elite" connections (her family is powerful) is being pushed for a role that you have earned through "Hard Merit."

options.text: "I respect the 'History' of your family, but this role requires specific 'Reliability' markers that I have spent years building. Let's let the 'Quality' of our recent results be the judge."
options.motivation: This is Commanding Respect. You don't attack her connections; you simply reinforce your own "Merit." You frame the competition as a "Quality Check." You look like a man of "Substance" who follows the "Social Contract" of hard work.

options.text: Complain to everyone that "the system is rigged" and she’s just a "nepo-baby."
options.motivation: This is "low status." It makes you look bitter and weak. You lose the Likeability Factor because you sound like a victim rather than a competitor.

options.text: Step aside and let her have it because you don't want to "fight a woman."
options.motivation: This is "submissive." In a merit-based "Arena," gender shouldn't be an excuse to give up your own "Dignity."

question_text: You see a senior male executive making a female peer very uncomfortable with "unverzorgd" comments or physical closeness in the "Public Arena."

options.text: Step in gracefully: "Hey [Name], we were just about to discuss the 'Quality' of that report—do you have a moment to step over here?"
options.motivation: This is Social Intelligence. You provide her with an "Exit" (Opening Doors) without starting a physical fight. You act as the "Dignified Protector" of the "Arena’s Harmony." It shows you have high-level "Navigation" skills.

options.text: Punch the senior executive to show how "Manly" you are.
options.motivation: You will be fired and possibly arrested. You’ve destroyed your "Professional Brand." A "Dignified Citizen" uses "The Velvet Tongue" before "The Iron Fist."

options.text: Pretend you didn't see it because he’s the boss.
options.motivation: This makes you "unreliable." If you don't protect the "Standard" of the Arena, you are complicit in its "Negative Quality."

question_text: A female peer in your professional/social circle expresses a romantic interest in you, but you do not feel the same.

options.text: "I’m very flattered, as I’ve always valued the 'Quality' of our connection. To maintain the 'Reliability' of our professional 'Arena,' I’d prefer to keep our focus on our 'Shared Merit'."
options.motivation: This is the Lasting Impression. You validate her "Dignity" (Likeability) but set a clear, "Solid" boundary (Commanding Respect). You protect her "Mirror" so she doesn't feel shamed. You emerge as a man of high character.

options.text: Laugh and tell her she’s "not your type."
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd." It’s cruel and destroys her "Dignity." You will be seen as "arrogant" by the whole circle.

options.text: Go on a date anyway because you are "too nice" to say no.
options.motivation: This is "submissive." You are creating a "messy" future conflict because you lack the courage to be a "Dignified Citizen" today.

question_text: You are being introduced to a new male colleague or partner. How do you set the tone for a "Quality" relationship?

options.text: Give a very light, delicate touch of the fingers to be "feminine."
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig" (submissive). It signals to him that you are a "Social Decoration" rather than a "Dignified Citizen." It closes the door to being taken seriously as a peer.

options.text: A firm handshake with direct eye contact, a warm smile, and: "It’s a pleasure to meet you, [Name]. I’ve been looking forward to our collaboration."
options.motivation: This is Graceful Authority. You establish "Peer Equality" immediately. By using his name and mentioning the work, you frame the relationship around Merit and Reliability. You look like a woman of substance.

options.text: Avoid the handshake and just give a small wave from a distance.
options.motivation: This is "afstandelijk" (distanced). It creates a barrier in the "Public Arena." A dignified woman isn't afraid to occupy her space in a professional greeting.

question_text: You are standing in a hallway or office talking to a male peer. He is standing a bit too close, making you feel crowded.

options.text: Back away nervously until you hit a wall.
options.motivation: This is "submissive." It shows him (and others) that you are easily "pushed" out of your space.

options.text: Maintain your "Solid Stance," take one deliberate half-step back to restore your "Dignified Distance," and continue the conversation with a clear, steady voice.
options.motivation: This is Commanding Respect. You reset the "Arena" without making a scene. By staying focused on the topic, you show that you are an "Adult" who manages her own boundaries with grace.

options.text: Put your hand on his chest and push him away while shouting "Back off!"
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd" (unpolished). It turns a small "Social Navigation" error into a "Public Arena" scandal. You lose your Likeability Factor.

question_text: A male peer constantly calls you "Sweetie" or "Hey girl" in the office or a group setting.

options.text: "I appreciate the friendliness, [Name], but I’d prefer we keep it professional to ensure the 'Quality' of our work environment. Let’s stick to [My Name]."
options.motivation: This is Graceful Authority. You correct the "Unverzorgd" behavior (Social Intelligence) but tie it to the "Quality" of the work. You look strong, reliable, and impossible to diminish.

options.text: Giggle and call him "Babe" back to show you can "take a joke."
options.motivation: This destroys your Professional Brand. You have just turned the "Public Arena" into a playground. You will not be trusted with "High-Merit" responsibilities.

options.text: Go to HR immediately without saying anything to him first.
options.motivation: In the middle-class matrix, a "Dignified Citizen" has the courage to set the boundary herself first. It shows you have the "Internal Strength" to lead your own life.

question_text: You are walking into a meeting room where five men are already talking.

options.text: Sneak in quietly and try to find a chair in the corner so you don't "interrupt" the men.
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig." You are treating yourself as an outsider. You lose your Authority before you even sit down.

options.text: Walk in with a "Solid Stance," scan the room, give a clear "Good morning, everyone," and take a seat at the main table.
options.motivation: This is the Belgian Superpower. You claim your place in the "Arena." By greeting the whole room, you act as a "Dignified Peer." You look like you belong there—because you do.

options.text: Run in and start talking loudly about how you couldn't find a parking spot.
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd." You are bringing "Chaos" into a "Professional Mirror." It makes you look "unreliable" compared to the calm room.

question_text: A male peer offers to carry your laptop or your bag for you as you walk to a meeting.

options.text: "I’ve got it, thank you. I value the 'Reliability' of my own setup, but I appreciate the gesture."
options.motivation: This is Opening Doors. You acknowledge his "Secret of the Gentleman" (Likeability) but maintain your own "Independence" (Dignity). You look like a woman who is fully in control of her "Merit."

options.text: "What, do you think I'm weak just because I'm a woman?"
options.motivation: This is "brash." You are turning a polite gesture into a "dirty" conflict. It kills your Likeability Factor and makes you look insecure.

options.text: Hand him everything and let him carry your coffee too.
options.motivation: This can look "onderdanig." If you want to be seen as a "High-Merit" peer, you should look like you are ready to work, not like you are on a "date."

question_text: You are meeting a male colleague or a new professional acquaintance for an informal drink. He is already seated when you arrive.

options.text: Walk up, stay standing until he rises to greet you, and then choose your seat gracefully.
options.motivation: This is Graceful Authority. By waiting a split second for him to rise, you activate a "Secret of the Notable"—reminding him to be at his best behavior. It establishes a "Professional Mirror" of mutual respect. You look like a woman of "Substance" who knows her value.

options.text: Wave from the entrance and shout "I’m here!" then run to the table and plop down.
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd" (unpolished). It’s too much "Chaos energy" for a Dignified Citizen. You lose your "Stance" and look like a student rather than a peer.

options.text: Sit down immediately and start complaining about how hard it was to find the place.
options.motivation: This is "Negative Quality." You start the interaction with a complaint, which kills the Likeability Factor.

question_text: You are at lunch with three male peers. The bill arrives and one of the men says, "Don't worry, we'll split it between the three of us, you don't have to pay."

options.text: "I value our 'Reliability' as equal partners. I’d prefer to contribute my 'Merit' share to the bill, thank you."
options.motivation: This is Commanding Respect. While the offer is "Gentlemanly," accepting it can subtly put you in an "onderdanig" (submissive) position in a professional context. By paying your share, you remain a "Dignified Peer" who is nobody’s debtor.

options.text: "Okay, thanks! I'll buy the next one!"
options.motivation: This is the Likeability Factor for social friends, but in a "High-Stakes" professional group, it can blur the lines. Be careful not to look like you are being "carried" by the men.

options.text: Get angry and lecture them about the gender pay gap.
options.motivation: This is a "Social Navigation" disaster. You’ve turned a friendly (if slightly old-fashioned) gesture into a "dirty" conflict. You look "difficult" rather than "dignified."

question_text: A group of male colleagues is debating where to go for a team dinner, but no one can decide.

options.text: Wait for them to finish and just go wherever they pick.
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig." You are a passenger in the "Arena."

options.text: "I know a place that offers excellent 'Quality' and a quiet 'Arena' for conversation. Shall I make a 'Reliable' reservation for us at 7:00 PM?"
options.motivation: This is Graceful Authority. You solve the problem (Social Intelligence) and take the lead (Commanding Respect). You look like a woman of action who values the group's "Quality" time.

options.text: Complain that all their ideas are terrible but offer no alternative.
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd." It’s "Negative Quality." A Dignified Citizen provides solutions, not just friction.

question_text: You are explaining an idea to a male peer in a social-professional setting, and he keeps finishing your sentences.

options.text: Stop talking and let him finish the whole story for you.
options.motivation: You are disappearing. You are letting him take your "Merit."

options.text: Wait for a breath, give a brief smile, and say: "You’ve got the spirit of it! To ensure the 'Quality' of the detail is clear, let me finish that thought..."
options.motivation: This is the Velvet Anchor. You acknowledge his enthusiasm (Likeability) but firmly reclaim your "Merit" (Dignity). You don't look annoyed; you look like an expert who is in control of her narrative.

options.text: Shout "Stop interrupting me!"
options.motivation: This is "brash." It creates a "Social Navigation" block. A "Notable" woman corrects with a "Soft Hand" but an "Iron Will."

question_text: A male peer has helped you with a difficult task or introduction.

options.text: "Thanks, you’re a lifesaver, I could never have done it without a man's help."
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig." It devalues your own "Internal Quality."

options.text: "I truly appreciate the 'Reliability' of your support. It added real 'Quality' to the project. I look forward to returning the 'Merit' soon."
options.motivation: This is Opening Doors. You acknowledge his "Substance" (Likeability) but frame yourself as a peer who will return the favor (Equality). You look like a "Dignified Citizen" who understands the "Social Contract."

options.text: Don't say anything because "it was just his job."
options.motivation: This is "afstandelijk." You miss the chance to build "Social Capital."

question_text: You’ve just bought a high-quality, reliable car or a premium piece of tech. A male peer notices and says, "Nice! That’s a serious upgrade. Did you get a big bonus?"

options.text: "I’ve worked hard for the 'Merit' results this year, and I wanted a tool that matches that 'Reliability.' It’s a very practical choice for my lifestyle."
options.motivation: This is Dignified Citizenship. You link the purchase to your "Hard Work" and "Utility." You aren't "opscheppen" (boasting) about the money; you are showing that you are a woman of "Substance" who invests in her own "Quality."

options.text: "Yes, I’m finally making enough to show everyone I’ve arrived at the top."
options.motivation: This is "Elite Arrogance." It can trigger a competitive response in male peers and kill the Likeability Factor. In the middle-class matrix, we celebrate the merit, not the ego.

options.text: "Oh, it's just a gift from my parents, I didn't really earn it."
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig" (submissive). It devalues your "Professional Brand." A "Dignified Citizen" owns her success with a steady hand.

question_text: You are at a "borrel" with male peers and the conversation turns to expensive hobbies (watches, cars, travel).

options.text: Try to "out-spend" them by listing even more expensive things you have bought.
options.motivation: This turns the "Social Arena" into a "dirty" competition. You look unrefined and "low status."

options.text: "I’ve always appreciated the 'Craftsmanship' behind [Topic]. For me, it’s about the 'Quality' of the experience rather than the brand name. What do you value most in your hobbies?"
options.motivation: This is Opening Doors. You pivot from "Price" to "Quality" and "Values." By asking for their opinion, you become a "High-Quality Listener." You look like a woman with "Refined Taste" who is also a great peer.

options.text: Stay silent and look annoyed because you think their hobbies are a waste of money.
options.motivation: This is "afstandelijk" (distanced). You miss a chance to build "Social Capital" by finding a "Shared Standard" of quality.

question_text: You are out for drinks with male colleagues and they have already paid for the first two rounds.

options.text: "I’d like to host the next round. I value the 'Quality' time we’re having, and I want to ensure our 'Social Contract' is balanced."
options.motivation: This is Graceful Authority. By taking your turn to pay, you reinforce that you are an Equal Peer. It prevents you from looking "onderdanig" and shows you are a woman of "Abundance" and "Reliability."

options.text: Let them keep paying because "men should always pay for women."
options.motivation: In a 19-30 professional "Arena," this can subtly lower your "Authority." If you aren't paying, you aren't a "Partner"; you are a "Guest."

options.text: Calculate exactly how much your one drink cost and put that specific change on the table.
options.motivation: This is "petty." It kills the "Likeability Factor." The "Dignified Citizen" deals in "Generosity," not "Accounting."

question_text: A male peer is showing off his expensive new watch or car in a way that feels a bit "opscheppen" (boasting).

options.text: "That’s a very 'Quality' choice, [Name]. The 'Reliability' of that brand is well-known. It certainly matches the 'Professional Mirror' you’re building."
options.motivation: This is Social Intelligence. You acknowledge his "Substance" (Likeability) without making it a competition. You look like the "Gracious Anchor" who is secure in her own success.

options.text: Tell him you saw the same one for half the price online.
options.motivation: This is "brash." You are attacking his "Dignity." It makes you look petty and creates a "Social Navigation" block.

options.text: Roll your eyes and walk away.
options.motivation: This is "afstandelijk." It’s better to give a brief, dignified acknowledgement and move the conversation back to "Merit."

question_text: You want to give a small gift to a male colleague who helped you with a big project (e.g., a bottle of wine or a book).

options.text: "I wanted to thank you for the 'Reliability' you showed on that project. This [Item] reflects the 'Quality' of work we did together. I hope you enjoy it."
options.motivation: This is the Lasting Impression. You link the gift to "Merit" and "Shared Success." It’s professional, generous, and maintains your "Dignity." You look like a woman of "Refinement."

options.text: Give him something very personal like a scarf you knitted yourself.
options.motivation: This can blur the "Privé en Zakelijk" boundary. In the professional "Arena," it’s better to stick to "Public" markers of quality (books, stationery, high-end consumables).

options.text: Don't give anything because you don't want him to think you are "interested" in him.
options.motivation: This is a missed opportunity for "Social Capital." A "Dignified Citizen" knows how to give a "Clean Gift" that is purely about professional respect.

question_text: You are in a meeting with male peers and a senior male client. The client looks at you and says, "Could you grab us some coffees while the men start the briefing?"

options.text: Stand up, take the orders, and bring the coffee with a smile to be "helpful."
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig" (submissive). You have just accepted a "Low Status" role in the "Arena." It will be very hard to reclaim your "Expert" status for the rest of the meeting.

options.text: "I’m actually here to lead the 'Quality' analysis on this project, but I’d be happy to show you where the machine is, or we can ask the office assistant to help so we can start the briefing together."
options.motivation: This is Graceful Authority. You firmly state your "Merit" (Commanding Respect) while remaining polite (Likeability Factor). You act as a "Dignified Peer" who is focused on the work, not the service.

options.text: Get angry and tell the client his request is sexist and offensive.
options.motivation: This is "brash." While the request was poor, an emotional explosion in the "Public Arena" can make you look "unreliable" to the rest of the group.

question_text: You are explaining a complex solution when a male peer talks over you to finish your sentence, but he gets the technical detail slightly wrong.

options.text: "I appreciate the enthusiasm, [Name]. To ensure our 'Reliability' stays at 100%, let me clarify that last point—the actual data shows [Detail]..."
options.motivation: This is The Velvet Anchor. You don't let the interruption stand, but you don't make it a "dirty" fight. You use "Quality Control" as your excuse to reclaim the floor. You look like the "Gracious Anchor" of the truth.

options.text: Stop talking and let him finish, then send an email later correcting him.
options.motivation: This is "submissive." You’ve lost the "Arena" moment. A Dignified Citizen corrects the "Mirror" in real-time with grace.

options.text: Roll your eyes and sigh loudly while he is talking.
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd" (unpolished). It’s passive-aggressive and lowers your "Social Intelligence" score.

question_text: A male peer or manager asks to meet you late at night or in a "Private Arena" (like his home or a hotel bar) to discuss a promotion.

options.text: "I value the 'Merit' of this discussion. To ensure we have the right 'Professional Mirror,' let’s meet at the office tomorrow morning or in a 'Quality' public café during business hours."
options.motivation: This is Social Intelligence. You set a "Solid" boundary (Dignity) without accusing him of anything. You protect the "Privé en Zakelijk" boundary, which reinforces your "Professional Brand" as a serious, reliable peer.

options.text: Go anyway because you don't want to "miss the opportunity."
options.motivation: This puts your "Reputation" at risk. In the middle-class matrix, the "Arena" matters. A promotion discussed in a "Private Arena" loses its "Merit" in the eyes of others.

options.text: Say "No way, that’s creepy" and hang up.
options.motivation: Too blunt. It creates a "Social Navigation" block. A "Notable" woman uses a "Velvet Refusal" that leaves her dignity intact and the professional relationship functional.

question_text: A male peer is trying to "out-work" you in a way that feels like a personal attack on your "Reliability." He is staying late and pointing it out to everyone.

options.text: "It’s great to see such 'Merit' energy. I prefer to focus on the 'Quality' and efficiency of my output during our 'Reliability' hours. Let’s see whose results serve the client best at the end of the week."
options.motivation: This is Commanding Respect. You don't get drawn into a "Time War." You pivot to "Quality" (Substance). You look like a secure, high-performing peer who isn't shaken by his "Performative" work.

options.text: Start staying even later than him just to "win."
options.motivation: This is "submissive" to his game. You are letting him define the rules of the "Arena."

options.text: Tell the boss that he is "trying too hard."
options.motivation: This is "underhanded." It makes you look threatened. A Dignified Citizen wins through her own "Masterpiece," not by sabotaging others.

question_text: A male peer has spoken poorly of your "Merit" behind your back, and you have heard about it.

options.text: Approach him privately: "[Name], I’ve heard there are questions about the 'Quality' of my latest work. I value our 'Social Contract'—let’s discuss the 'Merit' of those points directly so we can maintain a 'Reliable' partnership."
options.motivation: This is The Lasting Impression. You address the issue directly (Dignity) but stay professional (Social Intelligence). You look like a woman who cannot be "gossiped" away. You command respect by bringing the "Private" back into the "Public Arena."

options.text: Start a rumor about him in return.
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd." You are polluting the "Arena." You lose your "Likeability Factor" with everyone else.

options.text: Ignore it and hope he stops.
options.motivation: This allows your "Professional Mirror" to be smudged. A Dignified Citizen is the guardian of her own name.

question_text: You made a technical error in a report that a male colleague then presented to the board. The board is now questioning his reliability because of your data.

options.text: "I take full responsibility for the 'Quality' of that data. I provided the 'Reliability' check for [Name], and the error was mine. I have the 'Masterpiece' version ready with the correct figures now."
options.motivation: This is Radical Accountability. By stepping forward publicly to protect his reputation, you demonstrate immense "Internal Strength." You turn a failure into a display of high-level Graceful Authority. He will never forget your loyalty, and the board will see a leader in the making.

options.text: Let him take the blame since he was the one who didn't check your work before presenting it.
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd" (unpolished). It destroys the "Social Contract" of the team. You lose your Likeability Factor with your peers and look "unreliable" to the seniors.

options.text: Send him a private apology but stay silent in the meeting.
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig" (submissive). It leaves his "Professional Mirror" shattered while you hide. A Dignified Citizen clears the air in the same "Arena" where it was clouded.

question_text: A male peer is losing his temper with you in the office or a community meeting, speaking loudly and pointing his finger to intimidate you.

options.text: Lower your voice and slow your speech: "[Name], I value the 'Quality' of our collaboration, but I cannot navigate this 'Arena' while you are shouting. Let’s continue this when we can both maintain our 'Dignity'." Then walk away.
options.motivation: This is The Velvet Anchor. You set an "Iron Boundary" (Commanding Respect) without becoming "brash" yourself. By walking away, you reclaim the power. You look like the only "Adult" in the room, which naturally increases your status.

options.text: Shout back even louder so he knows you aren't afraid of him.
options.motivation: This is "Low Status" behavior. You have just descended into a "dirty" fight. The "Public Arena" now sees two "unrefined" people, and your Graceful Authority is gone.

options.text: Start crying so the other men in the room step in to protect you.
options.motivation: This is "submissive." While it might solve the immediate problem, you lose your "Expert" status. You want to be seen as a Peer, not a "Damsel."

question_text: A male peer has taken an idea you shared with him in a "Private Arena" (over coffee) and presented it to the boss as his own.

options.text: "I’m glad [Name] brought up that concept we discussed yesterday. To build on that 'Quality' foundation, I’ve prepared the 'Reliability' metrics for the next phase..."
options.motivation: This is Social Intelligence. You reclaim the idea by adding "Substance" that only the creator would have. You don't "attack" him; you simply outshine him with "Merit." You look like the true owner of the "Masterpiece."

options.text: Call him a thief in front of the boss.
options.motivation: This is "brash." It makes the boss uncomfortable and makes you look "difficult" to manage. You lose the Likeability Factor.

options.text: Say nothing and never share an idea with him again.
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig." You are letting your "Merit" be stolen. A Dignified Citizen protects her intellectual property with grace.

question_text: A male colleague asks you to "look the other way" or sign off on something "unverzorgd" (unpolished/illegal) because "we're friends."

options.text: "Our friendship is based on 'Quality' and mutual respect. I cannot compromise my 'Reliability' or the 'Weight of my Name' for this. Let's find a 'Merit-based' way to fix the problem correctly."
options.motivation: This is Commanding Respect. You use your "Relationship" as the reason not to do it. You set a moral standard that protects both of you. You look like a woman of Integrity—a "Notable" of the future.

options.text: Do it because you don't want to be "that girl" who ruins the fun.
options.motivation: Once you break the "Social Contract" of honesty, you lose your "Dignity" forever. If you are caught, your "Name" is worthless.

options.text: Report him to the authorities immediately without explaining why.
options.motivation: This is "afstandelijk." A Dignified Citizen gives a peer the chance to correct their own "Merit" before taking it to the "High Arena."

question_text: You are leaving a group or a project where a male peer has made things very difficult for you. You are having your final meeting.

options.text: "I’ve valued the 'Quality' of the challenges we’ve faced. I’m moving toward new 'Merit' goals, and I wish this 'Arena' continued 'Reliability' in the future."
options.motivation: This is The Lasting Impression. You leave with "Peace." By wishing them well (even the difficult peer), you prove that you are "Larger" than the conflict. You leave a "Clean Mirror," ensuring your reputation remains "High Status."

options.text: Use your final speech to list every annoying thing the male peer did.
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd." It makes you look bitter on your way out. People remember the "Ending" more than the "Middle." Don't let your exit be "dirty."

options.text: Just leave a post-it note and never come back.
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig." It lacks the "Stance" of a woman who owns her history.

question_text: A new, young colleague (aged 22) joins your team. How do you establish your "Dignified Authority" while remaining likable?

options.text: Treat them like a student and give them a long list of "rules" on day one.
options.motivation: This is "Elite Arrogance." It creates an unnecessary hierarchy that kills the Likeability Factor. You want to be a pillar, not a dictator.

options.text: A warm, steady greeting: "Welcome to the team. I value the 'Quality' of fresh perspectives—let’s find a time to discuss how your 'Merit' can best support our shared goals."
options.motivation: This is Graceful Authority. You acknowledge their value (Likeability) but frame the relationship around "Merit" and "Shared Goals" (Commanding Respect). You look like a secure, established leader.

options.text: Try to act like their "best friend" and use the latest youth slang to fit in.
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd." In your 40s, trying to look 20 ruins your Professional Mirror. It makes you look like you haven't mastered your own age group's "Dignity."

question_text: You are attending a high-level meeting. Unlike your younger years, you are now expected to lead the "Social Navigation" of the room.

options.text: Sit at the head of the table, lean back, and cross your arms to show power.
options.motivation: This is defensive body language. It can look "stiff" or "afstandelijk."

options.text: Sit upright, occupy your space fully, keep your hands visible, and scan the room with a calm, "Solid" gaze before speaking.
options.motivation: This is Commanding Respect. You project "Internal Peace." You look like someone who doesn't need to fight for attention because your "Substance" already fills the room.

options.text: Sit in the back and take notes silently like you are still an assistant.
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig" (submissive). At this age, silence is often interpreted as a lack of "Merit" or confidence. You are expected to be a "Pillar" of the conversation.

question_text: You are meeting a peer of your own age for a potential business partnership.

options.text: A brief, firm, dry handshake with a two-second eye-contact "check."
options.motivation: This is the Reliability Signal. At this age, you don't need to prove anything with a "bone-crusher" grip. A calm, steady handshake signals that you are a "Dignified Citizen" who is ready for "Quality" collaboration.

options.text: A long, intense handshake while you try to "stare them down."
options.motivation: This is "brash" and insecure. It’s "Low Status" behavior that suggests you are still trying to find your place in the "Arena."

options.text: A quick "high-five" or a fist-bump.
options.motivation: Too informal for the 31-50 "Established" group. It can blur the "Professional Mirror" and make the business deal feel "unverzorgd."

question_text: A neighbor asks you to join a local committee that you simply don't have the time for.

options.text: "I’m sorry, I’m just so busy, maybe next year when things are better?"
options.motivation: This is "weak" language. It suggests you aren't in control of your own schedule.

options.text: "I truly value the 'Quality' of this initiative for our community. However, my current 'Reliability' to my other pillars (work/family) means I cannot give this the 'Merit' it deserves right now."
options.motivation: This is Graceful Authority. You validate the project but set a "Solid" boundary based on your "Pillars." You look like a man/woman of integrity who knows their limits.

options.text: Just stop answering their calls until they stop asking.
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd." It ruins your reputation as a "Dignified Citizen" in the neighborhood.

question_text: You are heading into a busy week of meetings and family events. What is the 31-50 "Established" grooming standard?

options.text: Follow the latest trends (e.g., bleached hair or avant-garde fashion) to show you are still "relevant."
options.motivation: This can look like a "Crisis of Identity." It can distract from your "Substance."

options.text: A "Polished-Timeless" look: impeccably clean, well-fitted clothes, and a groomed appearance that says "Consistency."
options.motivation: This is Reliability. At this age, your look should be a "Background" for your ideas. You want people to trust your "Quality" of mind, and a "Verzorgd" appearance is the first step.

options.text: Let yourself go completely because "you've already made it."
options.motivation: This is "Negative Quality." It signals that you have lost your "Stance" and your discipline. It closes doors to high-level "Arenas."

question_text: You are at a restaurant with your children (or younger relatives) and they are being a bit "unverzorgd" (loud/messy).

options.text: Ignore them and keep talking to the adults; "kids will be kids."
options.motivation: You are failing in your role as a "Guardian of the Arena." This makes you look "unreliable" as a parent/leader to the other guests.

options.text: Quietly but firmly set the standard: "In this 'Public Arena,' we show respect through 'Quality' behavior. Let’s refocus so everyone can enjoy the 'Peace of the Table'."
options.motivation: This is Dignified Citizenship. You are teaching the "Social Contract." You look like a "Pillar" who manages their "Private Arena" with grace and authority.

options.text: Scream at them in front of the whole restaurant.
options.motivation: This ruins the "Social Arena" for everyone. You lose your "Dignity" by losing your temper.

question_text: You are walking through your neighborhood. You see several people you know only vaguely.

options.text: Head down, walk fast, and avoid eye contact.
options.motivation: This makes you look "arrogant" or "afstandelijk." You are wasting "Social Capital."

options.text: The "Established Nod": A calm nod, a brief eye contact, and a clear "Good morning" or "Good evening."
options.motivation: This is the Belgian Superpower. At this age, your greeting is a "Social Investment" in the stability of the neighborhood. It opens doors for future favors and builds a reputation as a "Dignified Citizen."

options.text: Stop everyone for a 10-minute conversation about your recent success.
options.motivation: This is "opscheppen." It kills the Likeability Factor. A pillar doesn't need to seek validation; they provide it.

question_text: A peer of your age has recently come into a lot of money and is being very "flashy" and loud about it.

options.text: Compliment them on their "Taste" and pivot back to "Substance": "That’s a bold choice. I’ve always found that the 'Quality' of the experience is what stays with us long-term. What’s your next 'Merit' goal?"
options.motivation: This is Social Intelligence. You don't judge their "Goud" (gold), but you remind them of the "Merit" of the middle class. You look like the "Gracious Anchor" who is secure in their own "Substance."

options.text: Tell them how much more money you have than they do.
options.motivation: This is "brash" and "low status." It turns the "Arena" into a "dirty" competition.

options.text: Stop talking to them because they are "unrefined."
options.motivation: This is "Elite Distance." A Dignified Citizen can navigate any level of refinement with grace.

question_text: You are invited to a gala, a high-level wedding, or a corporate ceremony.

options.text: Arrive 30 minutes late to show you are "busy" and important.
options.motivation: This is "unreliable." At this age, punctuality is the "Silent Handshake" of the "Established Pillar." It shows you respect the "Social Contract."

options.text: Arrive exactly 5 minutes early, groomed to the "Standard" of the event, and greet the hosts first.
options.motivation: This is Graceful Authority. You honor the "Arena" and the host. You look like someone who is comfortable in high-stakes environments and who understands the "Secret of the Notable."

options.text: Wear casual clothes to show you are "above" the dress code.
options.motivation: This is "arrogant." It signals that you don't value the "Quality" of the gathering.

question_text: You are leaving a professional or social group.

options.text: Slip away without saying anything to save time.
options.motivation: This leaves a "Void" in the "Social Mirror."

options.text: Make eye contact with the key people, give a "Solid" handshake or nod, and say: "Thank you for the 'Quality' time. I value our 'Reliability' together. Have a pleasant evening."
options.motivation: This is the Lasting Impression. You end on a note of "Dignity." At this age, your exit should be as clean and "Polished" as your entry. You leave the door open for decades of future "Merit."

options.text: Stay until the very end and start cleaning up the trash.
options.motivation: While helpful, unless it's a very close friend's house, it can blur your "Professional Mirror." A "Pillar" facilitates the exit with grace.

question_text: You are out for dinner with a mix of friends and new acquaintances. The service is exceptionally slow, and the group is starting to get restless and complain.

options.text: Call the waiter over and give them a "piece of your mind" so the group sees you are a person of action.
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd." Losing your temper makes the "Social Arena" feel dirty and tense. You look out of control, not in command.

options.text: Catch the waiter’s eye calmly and say: "We value the 'Quality' of your kitchen, but we’ve noticed a delay. What can we do to ensure a 'Reliable' flow for the rest of the evening?"
options.motivation: This is Graceful Authority. You solve the problem (Social Intelligence) without ruining the mood. By staying "Solid," you become the "Gracious Anchor" that the rest of the table relies on.

options.text: Sit in silence and keep complaining to your neighbor about the bad service.
options.motivation: This is "Negative Quality." It spreads the bad mood. A "Pillar" takes responsibility for the atmosphere of the table.

question_text: You’ve invited the neighbors over for a drink. Some are "Established Pillars" like you, and some are new, younger couples.

options.text: Spend the whole night talking to your old friends about the "good old days."
options.motivation: This is "Elite Distance." You are making the new people feel like outsiders in your "Arena." You are failing as a host.

options.text: Actively introduce the newcomers by highlighting their "Merit": "[Name], meet [Name]. They just moved in and are doing incredible 'Quality' work in [Field]."
options.motivation: This is Opening Doors. You are the "Social Weaver." By connecting people through their merits, you build a stronger, more "Reliable" community. You become the person everyone wants to know.

options.text: Tell the younger couples all the rules of the neighborhood they are probably breaking.
options.motivation: This is "arrogant." It closes doors immediately. A "Dignified Citizen" leads through example and warmth, not through "Policing."

question_text: A younger relative or colleague tags you in a "funny" but slightly unpolished or drunken photo on social media.

options.text: Leave it up to show that you are still "fun" and "down-to-earth."
options.motivation: This smudges your Professional Mirror. At age 40, your reputation is built on "Consistency" and "Substance," not on being the "Life of the Party."

options.text: Untag yourself and send a private, kind note: "I love our 'Quality' memories, but I prefer to keep my 'Public Arena' focused on my 'Professional Brand.' Let’s keep this one for the private album!"
options.motivation: This is Commanding Respect. You set a clear boundary (Dignity) but remain likable by framing it as a "Brand" decision. You look like a man/woman who is in total control of their legacy.

options.text: Get angry and report the photo as "harassment."
options.motivation: This is an overreaction. It kills the Likeability Factor. A "Dignified Citizen" handles these "Navigation" errors with a "Velvet Touch."

question_text: You are invited to an exclusive event where the people are much wealthier or more "Elite" than your typical circle.

options.text: Try to "fake it" by acting even more expensive than you are.
options.motivation: They will see through it immediately. It looks "forced" and "low status."

options.text: Maintain your "Solid Stance," be impeccable in your "Polished-Timeless" grooming, and listen more than you speak. Use "High-Quality" follow-up questions.
options.motivation: This is Dignified Citizenship. By being a "High-Quality Listener," you earn a place at the table without needing to shout. Your "Substance" is visible in your calm and your curiosity.

options.text: Stay in the corner and don't talk to anyone so you don't make a mistake.
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig." You are a "Pillar" of your own world; you have every right to be in any "Arena."

question_text: You are at a local sports club or community event and two people start a loud, petty argument near you.

options.text: Jump in and tell them both to shut up because they are being "low status."
options.motivation: You’ve just joined the "dirty" conflict. You lose your "Dignity" by becoming part of the noise.

options.text: Step in with a calm, low voice: "I think we all value the 'Quality' of this event. Why don't we take a 'Reliability' break and discuss this when the energy is a bit lower?"
options.motivation: This is Graceful Authority. You act as the "Social Anchor." You don't take sides; you protect the "Arena" itself. People will look to you as the natural leader.

options.text: Walk away quickly and pretend you didn't see it.
options.motivation: Sometimes necessary, but a true "Pillar" uses their "Social Capital" to maintain the peace of the community.

question_text: You have just completed a significant renovation or moved into a larger, higher-quality home. A neighbor or colleague asks, "So, I heard you moved to the big house on the hill. It must have cost a fortune!"

options.text: "It was a major 'Merit' milestone for us. It has the best of everything, from the kitchen to the smart home system."
options.motivation: This is "opscheppen" (boasting). It focuses on the "Price" and the "Power," which can create distance and jealousy. It kills the Likeability Factor.

options.text: "We are very grateful. It’s a very 'Practical' space for the family, and we really value the 'Quality' of the light and the quiet. It’s a solid 'Arena' for our next chapter."
options.motivation: This is Dignified Citizenship. You justify the home through "Family Utility" and "Atmosphere." You aren't showing off your bank account; you are showing your appreciation for a "High-Quality" life.

options.text: "It’s actually a total ruin and we’re broke now because of it."
options.motivation: This is "False Modesty." At age 40, people expect you to be successful. Lying about it makes you look "unreliable" or dishonest.

question_text: You’ve replaced your old family car with a premium, high-quality vehicle. A peer asks, "Is that the new [Brand]? Business must be booming!"

options.text: "I needed something with total 'Reliability' for the long commutes and family safety. I’ve found that investing in 'Quality' pays off in the long run."
options.motivation: This is Commanding Respect. You frame a luxury purchase as a "Logic-based Investment." You look like a man/woman who makes "Reliable" decisions for the right reasons.

options.text: "Yes, it’s the top model. I figured I deserved a reward for all those 60-hour weeks."
options.motivation: This is "needs-based" bragging. It suggests your "Merit" is tied only to the reward, not the work itself.

options.text: "It’s just a company car, I didn't have a choice."
options.motivation: This is "dismissive." A pillar should own their "Stance." If you chose a high-quality item, be dignified about why it’s a good choice.

question_text: You are out with a group of friends. Some are struggling financially, and you have had a very successful year. The bill arrives.

options.text: Pay the whole bill for everyone and say, "Don't worry, I made enough today to cover us all for a month."
options.motivation: This is a "Power Move" that creates "Social Friction." You make your friends feel like "charity cases," which destroys the peer-to-peer "Social Contract."

options.text: "I’d like to host the wine/appetizers tonight to celebrate our 'Quality' friendship. Shall we split the rest as usual?"
options.motivation: This is Social Intelligence. You share your success (Likeability) without taking away their "Dignity" as equals. You look like a "Gracious Anchor" who knows how to be generous without being "brash."

options.text: Insist on splitting everything exactly, even the water, to "be fair."
options.motivation: This is "petty" for someone in your position. A "Pillar" should be able to show a bit of "Abundance" without making it a spectacle.

question_text: You are attending a neighborhood garden party or a school event for your children.

options.text: Wear your most expensive suit and your flashiest watch to show the other parents you are the "Alpha."
options.motivation: This is "Elite Arrogance." You look "stiff" and unapproachable. It kills your Likeability Factor with other parents.

options.text: "Polished-Casual": High-quality chinos or a simple dress, neat shoes, and one "Notable" accessory (like a good watch or simple jewelry).
options.motivation: This is the Established Mirror. You look "Verzorgd" (neat) but "Accessible." You signal that you belong in the "High-Quality" circle without needing to compete for attention.

options.text: Wear old, stained gardening clothes to show you are "one of the people."
options.motivation: This is "unverzorgd." It shows a lack of respect for the host and the "Arena." A "Pillar" maintains a "Standard" even in casual settings.

question_text: You want to help a younger family member (in their 20s) who is just starting out, by giving them a significant gift (cash for a down payment or a high-quality tool).

options.text: "Here is the money. Make sure you remember that I’m the one who made your life easy."
options.motivation: This is "Elite Distance." It creates a debt that will ruin the "Likeability Factor" of the relationship.

options.text: "I value the 'Merit' you’ve shown in your first years of work. I’d like to invest in the 'Reliability' of your future—consider this a 'Notable' boost to help you build your own 'Quality' life."
options.motivation: This is Dignified Citizenship. You frame the gift as an investment in their "Merit." You preserve their "Dignity" by acknowledging their hard work first. You look like a true "Mentor-Pillar."

options.text: Make them sign a 20-page legal contract with 10% interest to "teach them a lesson."
options.motivation: While "Reliable," it lacks the "Grace" expected of an established pillar within a family "Arena."

question_text: A long-term business partner or a senior neighbor suggests a shortcut that is technically legal but feels "unverzorgd" (unpolished/unethical). It could save money but might smudge your reputation.

options.text: "I understand the 'Efficiency' you’re looking for. However, at this stage, the 'Weight of my Name' is tied to total 'Quality.' I’d prefer we find a solution that protects our 'Reliability' for the next ten years."
options.motivation: This is Commanding Respect. You use your "History" as a shield. By framing it as a long-term reputation investment, you look like a "Dignified Citizen" who cannot be bought. You inspire them to raise their own standards.

options.text: "Go ahead and do it, just don't tell me the details so I have 'plausible deniability'."
options.motivation: This is "unreliable." For an established pillar, "hiding" is a loss of dignity. If it fails, your "Merit" is destroyed, and you look like a coward.

options.text: Report them to the authorities immediately.
options.motivation: Too aggressive for a long-term partner. A "Pillar" tries to redirect the "Arena" back to quality first through "Social Intelligence."

question_text: Two colleagues or friends of your age are having a "dirty" argument in a public setting. Both are looking to you to take a side.

options.text: Pick the side of the person who is more "Elite" or powerful.
options.motivation: This is "onderdanig" (submissive). It shows you lack your own "Solid Stance." You lose the respect of both parties.

options.text: "You both bring 'Quality' points to the table. Let’s refocus on the 'Shared Merit' of our goal. I value the 'Reliability' of both your perspectives—how do we merge them?"
options.motivation: This is Graceful Authority. You act as the "Social Architect." You don't take sides; you elevate the conversation. You look like the natural leader who protects the harmony of the "Arena."

options.text: Tell them both to grow up and stop being so annoying.
options.motivation: This is "brash." It lacks the "Velvet Touch" required of an established pillar. It creates more friction rather than solving it.

question_text: A high-status "Elite" person is being condescending to a younger "Rising" professional in your presence.

options.text: Stay quiet so you don't offend the "Elite" person.
options.motivation: This is "submissive." You are failing in your role as a "Mentor-Pillar."

options.text: "I’ve been working with [Junior Name] and I’ve found their 'Merit' and 'Quality' of work to be excellent. In our circle, we value 'Reliability' over title, don't we?"
options.motivation: This is Dignified Citizenship. You use your "Status" to shield the junior. By subtly challenging the Elite person to meet your "Standard" of respect, you look incredibly strong and highly likable.

options.text: Join in the joking so the Elite person likes you more.
options.motivation: This is "Low Status" behavior. It shows you are insecure about your own position. It kills your "Likeability Factor" with the next generation.

question_text: You applied for a board position or a prestigious community role and were passed over for someone else.

options.text: Send an angry email listing all your years of "Merit" and why the decision is wrong.
